data.title: London’s housing crisis deepens as plans for 42,000 new homes scrapped
data.description:
Developers have mothballed at least 42,000 new London homes in a major blow to Sir Sadiq Khan’s hopes of ending the capital’s housebuilding crisis, according to new analysis.

At least 30,000 homes that had planning consent and a further 12,000 that had been allocated in local authority plans have been scrapped and switched to other uses, according to exclusive figures from Savills.

Katy Warrick, head of London residential development research at the property group, said anecdotal evidence suggested that the actual number could be as high as 50,000 homes.

The housing sites have largely been mothballed in the last three years, as residential developers have been struck by soaring regulations, high interest rates and low buyer demand.

Ms Warrick said these sites were now being used for warehouses, data centres, self-storage units, industrial sites and hotels, rather than homes.

It marks a major setback for the Mayor of London, who is grappling with a critical housing crisis in the capital and is failing to meet his homes targets by a significant margin.

The number of abandoned plans will be particularly concerning considering just how few homes received planning consent in London.

Slightly more than 20,000 homes got sign-off last year, a number that was already just a fraction of the 88,000 homes that London is supposed to build each year in line with Labour’s housing targets.

It follows claims that private housebuilding in the capital has been killed “stone-dead” by policies in recent years.

Lord Bailey of Paddington, a former Tory mayoral candidate, released figures last year showing London was building the fewest homes of any English region per capita.

Experts have claimed that new building safety regulations introduced in the wake of the tragic Grenfell fire are having a disproportionate impact in London, where a much higher share of new-build homes are in high-rise blocks.

High mortgage rates are also hammering buyer demand in the capital, making it less attractive to build new homes.

Key housing sites that have been lost include the Honeymonster Factory in Southall, which had planning permission for nearly 2,000 homes and is now owned by a developer that is instead planning to build a data centre.

John Lewis announced in February that it was closing down its housebuilding business, scrapping plans to build 1,000 homes across three sites, two of which were in London and one in the commuter belt, citing cost increases.

London-focused Berkeley Group announced it was halting all new land purchases earlier this month because of “unprecedented” increases in costs and regulation, as well as low buyer demand.

Ms Warrick said: “This has been slowly building over the last three or four years as the viability of residential development has been in a long, painful decline.

“When you bear in mind what the cost is and how complicated residential development is, it’s not worth it.”

Residential development was always a “slam-dunk”, best-value approach for developers before the pandemic, Ms Warrick said.

Since then, however, build costs, regulations, and interest rates have all soared. On top of this, the Government’s Help to Buy equity loan, which allowed buyers to purchase new build homes with 20pc government-backed equity loans that were interest-free for the first five years, disappeared in 2023.

Housing developers have claimed that these changes mean that the sums to build homes simply do not add up. Neil Jefferson, the chief executive of the Home Builders Federation (HBF), an industry group, said: “Viability is really becoming an issue.”

A spokesman for the Mayor of London said new emergency measures confirmed last month, including temporary relief from the community infrastructure levy, would ramp up building.

They said: “By reducing the demands on developers in the short-term, the measures will result in more homes including affordable homes being provided to those who need them most.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Heathrow’s new car parks to cost more than Gatwick’s second runway
data.description:
From HS2 to Crossrail, British infrastructure projects lead the world in one unenviable respect: their sizeable, often runaway budgets.

The bill for big-ticket construction schemes typically starts off with eye-watering sums – and then climb to even more staggering levels as budgets are blown and costs run rampant.

Now a plan to provide 36,000 parking spaces at Heathrow airport is poised to join the pantheon of cash-hungry mega-projects after it was revealed that it will come with a staggering £2.6bn price tag.

That’s £400m more than the estimated outlay for a new runway at Gatwick and more than half the cost of Heathrow’s Terminal 5 – the largest free-standing structure in the UK.

At £72,000 per parking space, the two multi-storey car parks planned under the scheme will also be comfortably the most expensive in Britain and a contender for the costliest ever built.

Heathrow argues the price per space will be lower because only half the overall cost, about £1.3bn, is for the buildings themselves, with the rest of the spending going towards associated infrastructure such as bus facilities.

Yet the airport has faced criticism from some of the world’s largest airlines, who will ultimately pick up the bill for the world’s most expensive car park.

In a week when National Car Parks (NCP), the country’s biggest car park operator, collapsed into administration, the plan might seem an ambitious one.

However, unlike NCP, which was hit by a drop in commuting, Heathrow can rely on a captive customer base and funding rules that mean construction costs are ultimately likely to be passed on as aircraft landing fees.

Airline bosses have attacked the project as typical of Heathrow’s largesse in lavishing cash on gold-plated enhancements that they and their customers must ultimately pay for.

The airport says the car parks will be a vital component of its £49bn plan for a third runway that will lift passenger numbers from 84 million a year to as many as 150 million.

It says the sites – one next to the M4 and the other to the south near the M25 – will be needed to cope with the thousands of additional passengers driving to what is already Europe’s busiest airport each day, even if a higher proportion of people take public transport.

And it argues that the expense of the project reflects construction costs in London that are themselves amongst the highest in the world.

But Luis Gallego, the boss of British Airways (BA) owner International Airlines Group, said the plan was overpriced.

He highlighted the shortcomings of a funding model that allows Heathrow to charge airlines a regulated fee to cover building and operating costs.

“We support expansion, but not at any cost. All future spending must be carefully controlled as Heathrow already charges passengers twice as much as Amsterdam and three times as much as Paris,” he said.

Willie Walsh, the director general of the International Air Transport Association, was blunter, describing the car parks plan as “madness” and saying his mind “boggled” at the £2.6bn bill.

He said Heathrow had proposed what would have been the world’s most expensive car park when he was chief executive of BA as part of a previous runway plan that was rejected in 2010.

He said: “You can find data on what it costs to build a car park, there’s lots of evidence there, and Heathrow is just way out of line with the rest of the world.

“The problem is that they are incentivised to spend as much money as possible and to build the most expensive car park and to be proud of that.”

Heathrow has said that calling the new structures car parks does not do them justice.

Thomas Woldbye, the airport’s chief executive, has instead referred to them as “parking villages”.

The blueprint for the new runway proposes “two large parkway car parks” that would replace more than 20 existing facilities, with the northern site able to accommodate 20,000 vehicles and the southern one 16,000.

It adds: “This could support fast and efficient connections to each terminal campus via dedicated transport systems.

“These new parkways could also provide local communities with improved access to airport terminals and public transport links and help reduce congestion on local roads.”

While a leaked Heathrow briefing paper states the cost of the parkways to be £2.6bn, a spokesman said the parking areas alone will consume only about half the budget, with the rest covering the cost of associated infrastructure such as bus facilities.

Major profit centre

The car parks may be necessary to cope with an influx of new passengers, but they would also be a major profit centre, with parking fees contributing £200m in revenue last year.

Heathrow’s long-stay car parks currently charge £46.80 for the first day and £37.40 for each additional 24-hour period.

Taking an average of £40 per day, the number of spaces planned could generate more than £500m in revenue over the course of a year.

That is not including income from drop-off charges and the short-stay car parks nearer to terminals that the airport has indicated it plans to retain.

Michael O’Leary, the chief executive of Ryanair, which does not fly from Heathrow, said drop-off fees were “a scandal” across UK airports and that parking had been turned into a cash cow integral to their business models.

He said: “The more they earn from car parks the more they will dividend it out to their shareholders and then complain that they can’t make a reasonable return on the risks they were taking investing in runways in London.

“But there’s no risk at all, it’s a complete freebie, which is why pension funds now own most of these airports.”

O’Leary said the cost of runways, car parks and terminals was ultimately the result of a “broken” regulatory model that “rewards inefficiency and incentivises airports to squander gazillions”.

A study from Oxford Global Projects commissioned by Heathrow Reimagined, a lobby group formed by airlines including BA, has suggested that Heathrow’s construction costs across a variety of infrastructure including car parks are consistently among the highest in the world.

The report said the cost of the runway alone – covering cement, asphalt and lights – would come in at more than £800,000 per metre, making it one of the most expensive ever built.

Jonathan Simcock, author of The Delivery Gap, an examination of failed infrastructure projects, said companies like Heathrow have an incentive to inflate estimated costs by “including all the bells and whistles they can”.

In so doing, he said, they aim to secure regulatory approval for levels of spending – in Heathrow’s case to be funded by airlines and their customers – that will cover all eventualities and “ensure they are not left holding the baby”.

“If the estimate is inflated then the company ends up making an inappropriate profit. But if it’s undercooked the company gets into trouble, somebody has to bail them out and the country doesn’t get the infrastructure it needs,” he says.

Woldbye has said that the costings for the car parks and the wider runway project could change and that Heathrow is open to conversations about modifying its plans.

However, Simcock warned: “When the conversation is about how it can possibly cost that much, very often it turns out that it should have been, can you really do it for that?

“For very large and highly complex HS2-type things – and Heathrow is big and complex – it is rare to get nice surprises and much more common to get nasty ones.”

data.title: The accident waiting to happen to Britain’s trillion pound pensions industry
data.description:
Success in public policy has been so thin on the ground in recent years that it is hard to point to a single example of it. But to my mind there is at least one exception: the idea of pensions “auto-enrolment”.

This came into force in 2012 after a lengthy Pensions Commission inquiry into shortfalls in retirement savings under the chairmanship of Lord Adair Turner, a former McKinsey high-flyer and director-general of the Confederation of British Industry.

The key recommendation was that employees would automatically be required to funnel a bare minimum of their earnings – matched by the employer – into a pension pot unless they deliberately opted out.

Even in conjunction with a much enhanced state pension, Turner’s initiative has come nowhere near solving the problem of deficient income in retirement.

But though in most cases the sums involved will fall a long way short of buying an annual cruise in the Caribbean, auto-enrolment has ensured that many more workers do put away at least something for later.

At the last count, the default fund for these savings – the state-backed National Employment Savings Trust (Nest) – had around £50bn under management.

This is expected to swell to at least £100bn by the end of the decade, a tidy sum which goes some way to addressing a long-standing structural weakness at the heart of the UK economy – that as a nation we save too little, but borrow and spend too much.

Plainly, today’s arrangements are not nearly as good as the system of private-sector final-salary pension provision that was once the norm, but that boat sailed a long time ago, and it’s not coming back. Auto-enrolment does at least go some way to filling the gap.

Unfortunately, this is where the plaudits end, for there is no mess quite so great as the various rules, regulations, and government interventions that surround pensions savings.

The propensity of ministers to interfere shows no sign of abating, and indeed has shifted up a gear or two under the current Chancellor, Rachel Reeves. Wherever there’s a pot of other people’s money to go after, you can bet your boots the Treasury has got its eyes firmly set on it.

Recent interventions come in three forms. First, the Government has further undermined some of the tax incentives for pension savings by capping National Insurance relief on salary sacrifice arrangements.

Second, it is making pension pots subject to inheritance tax where previously they were exempt.

And third, it is trying to force pension funds to invest in private, British assets such as infrastructure, private credit and private equity. 

I don’t altogether disagree with the patriotic intent of “mandating” pension fund investment for the supposed national interest.

It is obviously desirable that at least some of Britain’s pool of domestic savings gets ploughed back into UK productive assets. 

This used to be the case when the big final salary pension schemes ruled the land, but globalisation and government liability matching regulation destroyed all that, and in today’s world it is much more difficult to argue that pension money should be confined in the way proposed under legislation currently stuck in the House of Lords.

Imagine if our pension pots had been invested exclusively in the FTSE All-Share, UK gilts, or worse still, the grand folly of HS2.

You’d have missed out on one of the greatest bull markets of all time – America’s S&P 500. Sadly, much of today’s growth lies beyond these shores. It is wrong in principle to deny savers the opportunity to invest in it.

Ministers cannot magic up US levels of advancement simply by forcing savers to invest their money in UK assets. This is in any case putting the cart before the horse.

Britain should concern itself first with building an economy where people actually want to invest. Financial coercion is no substitute for the hard yards of wider economic reform.

Even in Britain, there really is no shortage of capital for worthwhile commercial investment. You scarcely need to mandate pension funds to provide it.

Yet where policy actively conspires to drive entrepreneurs and wealth away, and where it further forces savers to do what they wouldn’t voluntarily do, returns are bound to be disappointing.

But who needs coercion when you’ve got Nest? According to reports, trustees are lining up plans to invest up to 30pc of funds under management in private markets. The legislation requires only 10pc, with just half of that earmarked for UK assets.

Please don’t say Nest is under any pressure to do this from its masters in Downing Street. No sir, this is an entirely independent, arm’s-length decision.

All I can say is I’m glad not to be making it, and for that matter that I am not invested in Nest. The chances of things going wrong, disadvantaging Nest’s 13 million individual pension pots, are high.

Don’t take it from me. Just ask the Pensions Regulator, which has – embarrassingly for ministers – written to 58 schemes warning of potentially significant costs from undue exposure to private markets.

The regulator’s point is that when assets are private, they are both hard to value and even harder to sell when liquidation is required.

“Exiting private markets under pressure is less an orderly divestment and more a negotiated surrender of value,” Nikesh Patel, of investment management firm Van Lanschot Kempen, told the Financial Times.

Indeed so, as well publicised problems in US private credit markets have recently highlighted.

This is a world of “mark to fantasy”, as opposed to the realism of “mark to market”.

Surrounded by a veritable army of sponging, fee-hungry consultants, many trustees have no idea what they are doing and are being sold a pup.

It’s bad enough for savers when money managers do it voluntarily; just imagine the missteps when they are forced to do it under pressure from the Government in order to meet quotas.

The parallel is with the sort of misallocation of resources once seen at the Department for International Development when approaching the end of the financial year having underspent the budget. On a spend-it-or-lose-it basis, almost any Tom, Dick or Harry would be seen as worthy of a handout.

The investment bankers must be positively drooling at the prospect. According to Calvera’s well-known line in the Magnificent Seven: “If God didn’t want them sheared, he would not have made them sheep.”

I don’t want to be rude about a generally well-intentioned bunch, but the same might be said of many pension fund trustees. It’s an accident waiting to happen if ever there was one.

It’s not their fault. They are mere victims of a political mindset that believes economic growth can be conjured up by simply telling people how best to invest their money. As they are about to find out, that’s not how it works.

data.title: Morrisons to sell dozens of pharmacies in scramble to cut costs
data.description:
Morrisons has put dozens of pharmacies up for sale in a fresh effort to cut costs.

The private equity-owned supermarket has launched a sale process after concluding that many of its in-store pharmacies are not financially viable.

As a result of the review, property agents have been tasked with selling some of the pharmacies on a store-by-store basis, rather than the entire portfolio.

Those affected are likely to remain open in stores, but under the brand of any eventual buyer.

The move to sell dozens of pharmacies comes after Morrisons’ losses plunged to £381m last year, fuelled by steep borrowing costs.

Rising debt pressures have led bosses to reduce borrowings, which fell to £3.1bn from £3.5bn last summer.

The sale of pharmacies is the latest attempt to strip back the business and focus increasingly on traditional retail.

A separate restructuring last year triggered the closure of four pharmacies, alongside 52 cafés, 17 convenience stores, 35 meat counters and 13 florists.

This led to more than 300 jobs being put at risk of redundancy, as bosses identified areas “where the costs of operations are significantly out of line with usage, volumes, or the value that customers place on them”.

Rami Baitieh, chief executive of Morrisons, said the cuts were a “necessary part of our plans to renew and reinvigorate Morrisons”.

Morrisons is pursuing a turnaround strategy after Mr Baitieh said the business had been going through a “challenging period”.

The retail boss blamed weaker consumer spending among poorer shoppers, many of whom have been affected by price rises.

This has led to Morrisons struggling to boost its share of the grocery market, which is at 8.4pc.

That is up narrowly from 8.5pc this time last year, although it is well below the 9.9pc it achieved in October 2021, when it was bought by private equity firm Clayton Dubilier & Rice in a £10bn deal.

This has prompted speculation that Morrisons could lose its position as Britain’s fifth-largest supermarket to Lidl, which now boasts 7.7pc of the grocery market.

Morrisons is not alone in seeking to sell off pharmacies, as the sector has been battered by rising costs in recent years.

Around 1,400 pharmacies have shut in England since 2017, according to the National Pharmacy Association, leaving many households unable to access medicine in their areas.

This has been blamed on rising wage costs and a shortfall in how much the NHS pays pharmacies.

Boots has also closed hundreds of pharmacies in recent years, while LloydsPharmacy, which had 138 sites, collapsed in 2023.

Rachel Reeves has piled more pressure on pharmacies over the past year by increasing business rates and the minimum wage.

Supermarkets have also come under pressure from rising taxes under Labour, most notably by the Chancellor’s decision to increase employer National Insurance rates.

Speaking last month, Mr Baitieh pleaded with the Government to avoid hitting retailers with any extra costs.

He said: “I would say, please, no more. This is very important for the market. We expect inflation to go up.”

Morrisons declined to comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Rejoice! The English sports car is coming back
data.description:
Join me, if you will, for a stroll through time to the garage of memories at the end of the garden, where the pungent odour of motor oil mixes with mouldering grass cuttings.

In here you might find your father’s MGB roadster from 1968 – the one in Old English White with chrome bumpers, wire-spoke wheels and the black leather interior that reeked of Rothmans.

The canvas roof always leaked, but it was a joy to be in – even if its restrained good looks and rather limited power made it the sports car equivalent of a Rich Tea Biscuit.

Perhaps, however, the English sports car of yesteryear in your garage is the peerless Austin Healey 3000 from the 1960s – an aesthetic masterpiece, though not if it’s been rolled through a hedge (which is what my father did to his mother’s Healey after a binge at the George and Dragon in Epping).

Or how about the angular 1970s Triumph TR7? My Uncle William had one in burnt gold – which my cousin Andy used to rearrange a lamppost. William had it rebuilt. But you would.

Whichever old English sports car inhabits your own showroom of nostalgia, whether it’s one of the above or a grunty TVR Griffith or a lithe Lotus Esprit – like Roger Moore had in For Yours Eyes Only, or Julia Roberts floored in Pretty Woman – it’s a sad fact that while a few are still on the roads today, the companies that made them have either vanished or changed beyond recognition.

MG was mauled badly before being sold to the Chinese – who moved production to China in 2016. Triumph disappeared into the catastrophe of the demise of British Leyland. Lotus is owned by Geely, another Chinese giant, which announced it was cutting a third of the workforce – 550 jobs – at its HQ and factory in Norfolk last year. TVR exists and there is news of a new model… again.

Last time I heard Morgan, the venerable maker of wooden-framed sports cars from Malvern was thriving, albeit in the hands of a Continental industrial conglomerate, and then there’s Jaguar, which had the F-Type until it ceased production in 2024, though it was hardly the “spiritual successor” of the E-Type – a serious contender for the title of most beautiful sports car of all time. Aston Martin, the modern Bond’s wheels of choice, trundles on but is seemingly mired in commercial woes.

So it’s heartening that in these dark days, where good news is about as rare as a Resident Doctor on duty in England, that lovers of English sports cars have something to celebrate.

Because a new sports car start-up – Longbow – has unveiled a spankingly beautiful all-electric open-top “Speedster” model, which will do 0-60 in 3.5 seconds and allegedly go 275 miles on battery power. While the £84,995-Speedster is a rich-man’s toy – there’s no roof or windscreen – and is available only in a limited 150-model run (most of which are already sold), next year Longbow plans to begin making a Roadster model – a two-seater with a roof that will be priced at £64,000 and do 280 miles.

What’s more, they hope to make 5,000 of them a year with an entirely British supply chain, except for batteries which aren’t yet made here – but when they are, they will be.

If it all transpires, this will create and secure several thousand British jobs in the automotive sector. And it’s just the start: the firm would like to expand into bigger cars.

While I don’t think they have received a single handout from the Government, they do stand to benefit from Rachel Reeves’s economic policies because there are now quite a lot of unemployed automotive workers out there looking for new jobs.

England, says Longbow’s co-founder Mark Tapscott, who is ex-Tesla and BYD, is still the best place in the world to make a light-weight sports car, and what could be better if you’re embracing an electric future where every gram counts? Astonishingly, the Roadster weighs just 995 kilos, so about half of a Tesla Y or, funnily enough, about the same as my dad’s little old MGB.

Which is technologically impressive, and should give us all hope. Because while 5,000 cars a year – assuming they achieve it – is small beer compared with giants like Tesla, it’s a step in the right direction – and one that builds on a great English tradition of lightweight sports cars.

Not just that, it proves that despite the gloom, despite the burden of Labour’s taxes, regulation and energy costs, we can still do it. And, thank Heaven for that, because there’s no better way to explore the English countryside than behind the wheel of an English sports car. It’s what the Sussex Downs, the rolling hills of Hampshire and the Lake District were invented for.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: A banking crisis made in the Kremlin is gripping Russia
data.description:
The biggest lender to Russia’s defence companies is in free fall.

Two years ago, Promsvyazbank (PSB) was raking in cash as it bankrolled Vladimir Putin’s war machine. Now, four years after the Ukraine conflict began, it is haemorrhaging hundreds of millions of dollars.

In 2025 alone, it recorded a loss of 19.2bn rubles (£190m) after setting aside 300bn rubles to cover soured loans.

Typically, losses of this nature could be attributed to a flawed corporate strategy. However, many believe PSB is on the front line of a breakdown in Russia’s financial system as a defence-fuelled borrowing binge hits a wall.

Even beyond weapons makers, experts predict that Russia’s banks are grappling with a debt bomb lurking in the economy.

Vladimir Milov, an exiled opposition politician who was formerly a deputy energy minister under Putin, says: “We are already formally under the criteria of a banking crisis. The only thing that is missing is a bank run.”

Russian central bank data show banking sector profits plunged by 55pc to 176bn rubles in December.

Moscow Credit Bank, another major lender tied to the state oil giant Rosneft, also reported a loss in the final quarter of last year as it grappled with a surge in loan defaults.

At the start of February, the Centre for Macroeconomic Analysis and Short-term Forecasting (CMASF), a think tank aligned with the Kremlin, published a report stating that a “banking crisis has now been confirmed”.

This is based on the CMASF’s technical definition: that more than a tenth of banks’ loan books are unlikely to be repaid.

Businesses are only officially overdue on 3.3pc of their bank loans. But Russian central bank data show that the share likely to default soon is at 11.3pc, equivalent to 10.6tn rubles.

This has climbed from 10.4pc a year earlier.

Households are in even worse shape, with 13.3pc of retail loans deemed problematic.

However, when it comes to the threat of crisis, it is believed that the batch of bad business loans will pose the biggest problems for the Kremlin.

Since the start of Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, the Kremlin has covered the costs of its war largely by injecting massive amounts of cash into Russian companies through its banks.

This fuelled economic growth and created an illusion of Russian resilience, says Craig Kennedy, of Harvard’s Davis Center for Russian and Eurasian studies.

In particular, the Kremlin significantly watered down rules and credit checks for defence-sector lending, says Kennedy, who was formerly vice chairman at Bank of America Merrill Lynch.

“This has created a large pool of opaque, unmeasured and poorly managed default risk at the heart of the Russian banking system,” he warns in his Substack, Navigating Russia.

As of December, banks’ total loans to defence companies exceeded $200bn (£148bn), more than 23pc of banks’ corporate loan books. And this is a sector that has a notoriously bad credit history.

PSB’s losses reflect “deep troubles in the Russian military industrial sector”, says Milov.

But the vulnerabilities extend far beyond the defence sector, as the majority of businesses in Russia are in a painful downturn.

Putin has revealed that Russian GDP contracted by 1.8pc in January and February.

Three quarters of Russia’s industries are either underwater or flatlining, says Milov, with the coal, steel, paper and construction sectors all grappling with crises.

“You can imagine that most of the sectors of the economy are very weak in servicing their debts,” he says.

Unsurprisingly, the Kremlin is anxious.

In March, Putin told Russia’s oil and gas companies that they should use windfall oil profits triggered by the war in Iran “to reduce their debt burden and pay off their debt to domestic banks”.

Elvira Nabiullina, the Russian central bank governor, warned last June that the resources that had fuelled two years of economic growth in Russia – namely banking capital reserves and Russia’s national wealth fund – were “truly exhausted”.

In response to the looming blow-up, the central bank has already announced a fleet of measures to tighten lending rules.

Nabiullina said in March that the central bank would stop banks from assessing corporate loans “on the assumption that a borrower will receive government support even if there are no solid grounds for that”.

This advice highlights the nature of the problem facing the Kremlin.

The CMASF’s definition of a “banking crisis” does not mean that Russia’s financial institutions are collapsing, says Milov: “It means that we are on the verge of a situation where banks will soon arrive at the government’s doorstep asking for major bailouts.”

Tom Keatinge, the director of the Centre for Finance and Security at the Royal United Services Institute, adds: “The country is in a banking crisis, there’s no question – but a banking crisis is only as serious as the state allows it to be.”

Russia’s national wealth fund injected around $10bn into several leading banks last year.

“In effect, the central bank prints money to support them,” says Elina Ribakova, a senior fellow at the Peterson Institute for International Economics.

Strain on the banking sector is already leading to lower lending. Russia’s combined war funding slumped by 16pc in 2025, driven by less borrowing among defence firms, according to Kennedy.

Central bank data also showed consumer lending contracted by -0.7pc in 2025, a stark shift from the 11.3pc growth recorded in 2024.

For now, soaring oil and gas prices are bringing some solace, resulting in higher revenues for Russia’s energy giants since war broke out in the Middle East.

This has already led some economists to predict that it will help the Kremlin skirt a recession in the second half of this year.

But Putin is on borrowed time if Donald Trump strikes a peace deal in Iran and the global energy crisis falls away.

“The numbers all look good as long as the oil price is high,” says Ribakova.

“If it goes down, you’ll have these sprawling quasi-government institutions everywhere that will suddenly have problems and will come onto the balance sheets of the central government.”

If oil prices crash to $40, the Russian government would suddenly face the prospect of needing to recapitalise state energy giants Gazprom and Rosneft and its banks, says Ribakova.

“It would not be small numbers. If you look at past financial crises, it easily gets to 10pc to 20pc of GDP.”

All of which suggests that it won’t take much for the banking crisis gripping Russia to develop into something much more serious.

“The underlying problems are multiplying and at some point, all of this explodes at once,” says Milov. “2026 might be a breaking point.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Founder of Chinese property giant pleads guilty to fraud
data.description:
The founder of the failed Chinese property giant Evergrande is facing the threat of life in prison after pleading guilty to fraud and bribery.

Hui Ka Yan, also known as Xu Jiayin and once one of China’s richest men with a fortune of $45bn (£36bn), made the plea during a court hearing in Shenzhen earlier this week.

It marks a significant fall from grace for the businessman, who turned Evergrande into China’s biggest property developer before it collapsed under the weight of huge debts in 2021.

Evergrande’s unravelling has led to a raft of claims against Hui, including embezzlement of assets and illegal fundraising. The offences carry a maximum penalty of life imprisonment and confiscation of property.

The court in Shenzhen said it would announce its verdict on the case at a later date, claiming on Tuesday that Hui had “pleaded guilty and expressed remorse” during proceedings.

His trial took place after he was detained in China in Sept 2023 on suspicion of committing crimes. Hui, a former steelworker, has not been seen publicly since.

At its peak, Evergrande had a stock market valuation of more than £37bn, working on around 1,300 projects in 280 cities across China.

However, large debts crippled the company in 2021, sending the company’s value plummeting and sparking China’s property downturn, which has weighed on the economy for years.

The China Securities Regulatory Commission found in 2024 that Evergrande had inflated earnings and committed securities fraud.

The commission found that revenues were overstated by more than 560bn yuan (£60.6bn) by recognising sales in advance.

It fined Hui 47m yuan (£5.1m) and handed him a lifetime ban from participating in China’s stock markets.

The indebted property giant was fined 4.18bn yuan (£451.4m) before being ordered to liquidate by a Hong Kong court in 2024. The company was removed from the Hong Kong stock exchange last year.

The collapse of the company has fuelled a property crisis across China, as home sales slowed and construction companies scaled back developments.

Beijing has sought to prop up the property market by providing incentives to prospective home buyers.

However, the economy has since been dealt a fresh blow by the Iran war, straining China’s energy supplies and piling pressure on the country’s manufacturing sector.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: GB News accuses Labour MP of spreading populism ‘conspiracy theory’
data.description:
The chairman of GB News has accused a senior Labour MP of peddling a conspiracy theory over claims the channel is a mouthpiece for populism.

Alan McCormick, a partner at GB News co-owner Legatum, has criticised Liam Byrne after he claimed the broadcaster’s investors were “volatility traders” hoping to cash in on chaos in Britain.

Writing in The Telegraph, Mr McCormick said the claim “verges on conspiracy theory”, adding: “It demonstrates an ignorance about how the media works.”

Mr Byrne, who chairs the House of Commons’s business and trade committee, has accused GB News’s backers of seeking to promote populist politicians to unleash market chaos they can profit from.

The Labour MP commissioned research for his book, Why Populists are Winning and How to Beat Them, that found more than £170m had been handed to media organisations, politicians and think tanks aligned with the populist Right over the last five years.

In a robust rebuttal, Mr McCormick dismissed claims of political bias at GB News as “nonsense” and insisted the channel provided a platform for a more diverse array of opinions than any of its rivals.

He wrote: “The money invested in GB News by its investors has also been wildly mischaracterised in various places as being politically motivated. This is simply not the case.

“GB News was founded to surface the topics the British people want to talk about and the ideas that work, taking it off the political continuum, and not aligned with any political party.”

In response, Mr Byrne told The Telegraph: “Well, he would say that, wouldn’t he?”

He added: “Ofcom know exactly what they’re supposed to be doing to impose some proper rules at GB News – they need to get on and do it.”

It marks a rare intervention by Mr McCormick, who was appointed as chairman of GB News in 2022 following the abrupt departure of founder Andrew Neil. Dubai-based hedge fund Legatum co-owns the channel alongside the hedge fund tycoon Sir Paul Marshall.

The investors have pumped more than £140m into the loss-making channel since its inception five years ago. The latest accounts for the year to March 2025 show they injected a further £17.7m as losses narrowed to £22m.

GB News has repeatedly surpassed the BBC News channel and Sky News for audience numbers while revenues have grown as the channel shrugged off an advertising boycott that dogged its early years.

But the broadcaster has been embroiled in repeated controversies over impartiality, including its use of politicians such as Nigel Farage and Sir Jacob Rees-Mogg as presenters.

The channel was fined £100,000 by regulator Ofcom in 2024 for breaking impartiality rules in a programme featuring Rishi Sunak, who was the prime minister at the time.

It has also previously been censured for airing conspiracy theories linked to Covid-19 vaccines.

However, GB News scored a major victory over the regulator last year, when the High Court overturned a ruling that the channel had breached the broadcasting code in a programme presented by Sir Jacob.

Ofcom subsequently planned to tighten its rules to block politicians from acting as presenters on any show on TV or radio, but dropped the proposals in the face of fierce opposition from GB News and Rupert Murdoch’s News UK.

The picture Liam Byrne paints verges on conspiracy theory

By Alan McCormick, the chairman of GB News

Why would anyone invest in a broadcast news channel today? In our age of streaming and our attention-based economy, combined with declining television consumption, it may not seem like a growth industry. What’s more, the “media mogul” is hardly the most universally loved of stock characters.

Yet that is where Legatum, the private investment firm in which I am a partner, happily finds itself in 2026. Alongside Sir Paul Marshall, we invested in All Perspectives Ltd, the parent company of GB News, in 2021 when the channel launched.

The main reason we invested is very simple. It’s the same reason that investors back almost any project: we believed it would be a sound commercial enterprise. And more, we believed it would meet an audience need that was unmet by the incumbent providers.

Five years later, that judgment has been thoroughly vindicated – GB News is Britain’s number-one news channel.

As of March 2026, it has beaten both the BBC and Sky in seven of the last eight months, both in average audience and audience share.

In February, it had an average audience of 99,000 viewers, up 13pc on the previous month and far surpassing the BBC’s 78,400. Revenues have increased 65pc in the last 12 months, and while the company has been loss-making, we are patient and confident that in time the business will prosper.

Far from making the most of what remains of a declining industry, GB News is at the cutting edge of media technology. GB News is a technology platform, having pioneered hardware that is now being used by the rest of the industry. Visit one of our studios and you will find a slick, automated operation which embraces new tech and AI unencumbered by legacy broadcast hardware and practices.

Why did we believe that GB News would prove to be a sound investment? There is a clue in the name of the channel’s parent company: All Perspectives. To us, it was evident that a major gap existed in the media marketplace for voices that were routinely ignored by legacy broadcasters – not fringe voices, but those of normal communities all across Britain.

The decline of broadcast media cannot be solely blamed on the rise of the internet and streaming. It is also a product of people switching off because the stories which matter to them have been ignored by broadcasters for a very long time. Viewers simply didn’t want what the established channels were selling.

This judgement has been vindicated. Viewers are tuning in to GB News in droves, largely because they finally hear their perspectives being taken seriously and given airtime. And yet they are also exposed to genuine debate, making nonsense of suggestions that the channel is a mouthpiece for a particular political agenda.

GB News guests and panels showcase far more diversity of opinion than its competitors. Rather than the carefully choreographed and contained “discussions” found on other channels, GB News puts those with opposite views into conversation with one another, and allows viewers to decide for themselves.

Some people have their own ideas about why we would make such an investment. In the course of promoting his new book on so-called populism, Liam Byrne MP has suggested that we, investors in GB News, are “volatility traders”, planning to disrupt Britain in order to profit from future chaos by making big bets on the markets.

Investors certainly do plenty of sophisticated, long-term thinking, but the picture Mr Byrne paints verges on conspiracy theory. It demonstrates an ignorance about how the media works. While there are certainly easier and speedier ways to drive returns, we view this as a very significant, long-term investment in British journalism.

The money invested in GB News has also been wildly mischaracterised in various places as being politically motivated. This is simply not the case. GB News was founded to surface the topics the British people want to talk about and the ideas that work, taking it off the political continuum, and not aligning with any party.

The investors behind GB News have always been guided by the value of competition and free enterprise, which is a fundamental British ideal. That applies to our investment in a media company as much as any other project. We sincerely value an open media landscape, one in which genuinely diverse views compete on a level playing field.

News channels should be free to present their very best broadcasting, to win viewers to their conclusions, and to generate revenue whilst they do so.

Such a liberated media landscape is what many critics of GB News say they want too. If they are as sincere about that as we are, then GB News’ success should be welcomed by its competitors and critics as much as by its rapidly growing audience.

data.title: Channel 4 programme chief quits amid shake-up at broadcaster
data.description:
Channel 4’s veteran head of programmes is to step down amid a leadership overhaul at the youth-focused broadcaster.

Ian Katz, the chief content officer, will leave in the autumn after nearly nine years in the role, making him the longest-serving programme boss in the channel’s history.

Mr Katz, a former BBC Newsnight editor and senior journalist at The Guardian, joined Channel 4 in 2018. He is credited with bringing shows such as Taskmaster to the channel, while he also oversaw the Russell T Davies drama It’s A Sin and documentaries including Russell Brand: In Plain Sight.

The 58-year-old was also responsible for driving Channel 4’s expansion on YouTube as the broadcaster tries to navigate a decline in traditional TV viewing and exodus of young audiences to streaming rivals.

However, Channel 4 has faced criticism in recent years that it has lost its edge and now relies too heavily on old formats such as Gogglebox and The Great British Bake Off. The channel was launched under Margaret Thatcher in 1982 with a remit to make challenging and provocative shows.

Mr Katz’s departure comes a month after Priya Dogra took over as chief executive of Channel 4. Ms Dogra was selected to replace Alex Mahon, who left last year after successfully fending off attempts to privatise the broadcaster.

The former Sky and Warner Bros executive will now seek to shape her new leadership team after a period of uncertainty for Channel 4, which lost both its chairman and chief executive last year.

Sir Ian Cheshire, the previous chairman, stood down at the end of his term last April and was appointed this month as the new chairman of regulator Ofcom.

Jonathan Allan, Channel 4’s former chief operating officer, who was in the running for the top job, also stepped down earlier this year.

Ms Dogra said: “Ian has been an outstanding creative leader for Channel 4 over nearly nine years – the channel’s longest-serving head of programming.

“He has overseen an era of creative renewal, delivering bold and distinctive public service programming with intellectual rigour, good humour and an unmistakably Channel 4 glint in the eye.”

The leadership shake-up comes as Channel 4 seeks to carve out its role in the streaming era. The broadcaster is spending less on making programmes as it grapples with declining advertising revenues from its traditional TV business.

Channel 4 has been striking closer partnerships with its rivals as public service broadcasters seek to build scale to fight back against the likes of Netflix. But it is also contending with a wider reshaping of the TV landscape, with Sky locked in talks to buy ITV’s broadcasting business.

Mr Katz said: “It’s been a privilege beyond words to lead Channel 4’s talented and passionate commissioning team through such a transformative period.

“I’m incredibly proud of what we’ve achieved, from pioneering the evolution of a commercial public service broadcaster into a digital streaming business to backing groundbreaking programmes and talent that have brought a bit of joy to audiences’ lives.

“Above all, I’m proud that Channel 4 has continued to be as disruptive and impertinent as ever, asking the questions and covering the stories that other broadcasters and streamers won’t.”

Mr Katz will remain in his role until October to commission and oversee the channel’s slate of programming for 2026.

data.title: US is best place to invest, says boss of British drugs giant
data.description:
The boss of Britain’s largest drug developer has praised the US as the best place to invest amid fears over the waning appeal of the UK.

Dame Emma Walmsley, the outgoing chief executive of GlaxoSmithKline (GSK), said it was delusional to suggest Britain could become a competitor to the US. She lauded America as key to the group’s future growth.

“It’s more than half of GSK’s global turnover today,” she said of the US. “It’s still the leading market in the world in terms of the launches of new drugs and vaccines. It’s still, probably alongside China … the best market in the world to do business development.

“No one should be deluded that the UK is going to be a massive scale market, a domestic market, but it can be an exporter of innovation in life sciences.”

Dame Emma said GSK, which spends £1bn developing new drugs in the UK every year, is preparing to invest $30bn (£23bn) in the US by the end of this decade.

She is set to leave GSK at the end of the year after eight years in charge.

‘Worst country in Europe’ for drug prices

Her comments are likely to fuel fears about the attractiveness of Britain’s pharma sector after several major drugmakers cancelled UK projects worth billions – amid concerns over NHS drug price rules and pressure from Donald Trump, the US president.

AstraZeneca recently announced it was pausing a planned £200m investment in a Cambridge research facility, while Merck scrapped plans for a £1bn research centre in London and laid off 125 scientists.

In September, Eli Lilly, the American manufacturer of weight loss drug Mounjaro, also said that it was reconsidering plans for Gateway Labs, its London-based hub that provides support for biotech startups.

The UK Government has struggled to revive overseas interest in Britain in recent years, with the number of foreign direct investment projects falling 12pc last year to a record low of 1,375, according to official figures.

High energy costs, recent pressures like the increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions and wider geopolitical uncertainty have all been cited as reasons for declining investment in the UK.

One of the key concerns of Britain’s pharmaceuticals industry has been over NHS drug pricing in the UK. 

After postponing its investment, Dave Ricks, the chief executive of Eli Lilly, said the UK was “probably the worst country in Europe” for drug prices.

The Government agreed to a deal with the US at the start of the month to scrap tariffs on UK drug shipments – in a bid to end the row with Britain’s pharmaceutical industry. 

Dame Emma welcomed the deal as “a step in the right direction” for Britain.

The agreement means the UK will pay more for medicines through the NHS, in return for a guarantee that US import taxes on pharmaceuticals made in Britain will remain at zero for three years.

data.title: Britain at risk of mobile signal rationing as energy costs soar
data.description:
Mobile network operators have warned they could be forced to ration phone signal access to combat soaring energy prices triggered by the war in Iran.

Telecoms giants including VodafoneThree, Virgin Media O2 and BT-owned EE have issued a stark warning to the Government over energy costs after being excluded from Rachel Reeves’s support scheme.

Some companies are understood to be drawing up contingency plans as they grapple with heightened power costs.

These include rationing access to their networks or slowing down speeds to help reduce energy use. Another option is surge pricing, which would charge customers an additional fee at peak times.

Mobile phone services such as calls and internet data could be the worst affected, although broadband could also be hit.

Bosses warned that rising energy costs could also force the companies to scale back plans to expand their 5G networks, resulting in jobs either being cut or moved offshore.

In response to the threat, a Government spokesman said mobile network operators are required by law to maintain connectivity.

Industry executives are frustrated at Labour’s decision to exclude telecoms companies from a recent support package aimed at shielding businesses from soaring energy costs.

The Chancellor unveiled plans to cut electricity bills for 10,000 manufacturers by up to 25pc under the British Industrial Competitiveness Scheme (BICS) last week, though the measures will not come into effect until April 2027.

Telecoms companies believe their sector warrants support as an operator of critical national infrastructure.

One source branded it a “serious oversight”, adding that it raised questions about which parts of the economy the Government was prioritising.

Mobile networks are highly energy-intensive, consuming just under 1 terawatt-hour of electricity each year. That is the equivalent of powering 370,000 homes a year.

Operators hedge their energy costs to help weather price spikes.

However, prices have still increased by 70pc in recent years as a result of Russia’s invasion of Ukraine and the outbreak of war in the Middle East, which led to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, the crucial trade artery that transports a fifth of the world’s oil and gas supplies.

The price of electricity is mostly set by gas, which has risen by 33pc since the start of the war in Iran.

Telecoms bosses argue the sector is disproportionately exposed to the crisis due to the “always on” nature of mobile networks, meaning they are unable to shift demand to cheaper, off-peak times.

Any move to ration or slow down access to mobile signals, which is understood to be a worst-case scenario, would be a major blow to Britons already frustrated by patchy coverage. The UK ranks bottom of the G7 for 5G download speeds.

It could also deal a setback to the UK’s sluggish economy, with connectivity estimated to be worth £6.6bn per year.

Ms Reeves is already facing a backlash from other business leaders over her BICS measures, which they say do not go far enough and would arrive too late to prevent job losses.

A spokesman for VodafoneThree said: “We are disappointed that the Government has chosen not to include the telecoms sector in the British Industrial Competitiveness Scheme.

“We urge the Government to consider the impact of rising energy prices on the vital telecoms sector that unlocks growth in all parts of the economy.”

BT and Virgin Media O2 said rising energy prices could hinder long-term investment in the UK, but said they had no plans to ration access to their networks.

data.title: Law firm that charges $3,000 an hour caught using AI
data.description:
One of the world’s most expensive law firms has apologised for using artificial intelligence to draft legal documents in a high-stakes court battle.

Sullivan & Cromwell, which charges its clients up to $3,000 (£2,225) an hour, has admitted using AI to write a flawed submission to a New York court.

In a letter to the judge, the firm blamed AI “hallucinations” for a document that referred to fake cases and “non-existent legal sources”.

Sullivan & Cromwell, which is classed as one of America’s prestigious “white-shoe” law firms, said AI protocols were not followed despite “comprehensive policies and training requirements”.

The letter said that lawyers at Sullivan & Cromwell are required to complete two mandatory training modules before being allowed to access AI tools.

The firm, which is representing Donald Trump in his appeal against a criminal conviction, said that lawyers are instructed to “trust nothing and verify everything” from AI.

It says the training repeatedly emphasises the risk of hallucinations.

“We deeply regret that this has occurred,” said the letter, signed by Andrew Dietderich, Sullivan & Cromwell’s head of restructuring.

The controversy emerged as part of an ongoing case involving Prince Global Holdings Limited, a Cambodian conglomerate whose founder, Chen Zhi, is currently facing criminal charges for his alleged involvement in crypto scams.

Sullivan & Cromwell is representing liquidators appointed by authorities in the British Virgin Islands, where the Prince Global conglomerate is incorporated.

The mistake was spotted by lawyers from rival firm Boies Schiller Flexner, which is representing Mr Zhi and his conglomerate in the ongoing court battle.

The US department of justice has accused Mr Zhi of running compounds where individuals held against their will are forced to carry out cryptocurrency investment fraud schemes, known as “pig butchering” scams

In coordination with their US counterparts, British authorities sanctioned Mr Zhi in October 2025 and froze his UK assets, which included a £12m mansion on one of London’s most expensive streets and a £100m office building in the City.

The same month, US authorities separately seized around $15bn worth of Bitcoin from Mr Zhi.

In January this year, the 37-year-old billionaire was extradited to China from Cambodia, where he is expected to face multiple charges.

Sullivan & Cromwell and Boies Schiller Flexner were contacted for comment.

data.title: Denmark’s fat jab champion is slimming down the whole economy
data.description:
Novo Nordisk, Denmark’s fat jab pharmaceutical giant, reached an unwelcome milestone last week.

After a dizzying 76pc plunge in its share price over the past 18 months, a torrid week of trading meant it finally gave up the last of its market gains recorded since June 2021 when it released pioneering weight-loss drug Wegovy.

It follows years in which Novo Nordisk has been arguably Denmark’s greatest global success story. Its first-mover success with Wegovy and Ozempic turned it into Europe’s largest company, with the $100bn (£74bn) weight-loss drugs market at its feet.

The tide has since turned. This week, Novo admitted that its newest drugs were showing less promise in trials than those of its American rival Eli Lilly. It has also yielded to Donald Trump’s demand for hefty price cuts in the hitherto lucrative US market.

Novo is now in a metaphorical weight-loss programme of its own. It has shed its long-serving chief executive, cut 11pc of its workforce and lost hordes of foreign shareholders.

This is not a typical tale of corporate tumult. Novo is such a colossus in Denmark that its ructions can shake the country’s whole economy.

Yet Mette Frederiksen, the country’s Left-wing prime minister, chose this moment to take the country to an early election, set for March 24.

Since taking office in 2019, Frederiksen has steered Denmark through the turbulence of the pandemic, the Ukraine war and Trump’s threats to seize Greenland.

She will be hoping that Novo’s travails do not derail her bid for a third straight election victory.

It is something of a gamble. Novo’s fate is inextricably linked to that of the economy and thus to her own political prospects.

The company sold 309bn krone (£36bn) of drugs last year, which is more than 10pc of Denmark’s annual $430bn GDP. It employs one in every 100 Danes.

As Novo’s crisis deepened, Denmark’s economy – which was one of the fastest-growing in Europe – slowed dramatically at the end of last year. The country recorded 2.2pc growth in the third quarter, falling to just 0.2pc in the fourth.

Per Hansen, an economist at bank Nordnet, says: “One of the engines that has been driving the Danish economy over the past two to three years has been Novo Nordisk and the pharma industry.”

Las Olsen, the chief economist at Danske Bank, says that if you remove the pharmaceuticals sector, Denmark’s growth rate would be more like 1pc or 1.5pc.

“Which is fine, of course – but not the stellar growth rate that we have seen in the headline numbers,” he says. “I think we will grow more in line with European economies, perhaps a little less.”

These aren’t views that will cheer up the gloomy Danes.

Measures of Danish consumer confidence have languished in deeply negative territory ever since Russia invaded Ukraine.

“It’s a conundrum,” says Helge Pedersen, the chief economist at Nordea Bank. “House prices are at a record high, there’s almost no unemployment – everything is good but consumers are depressed.”

He says ordinary Danes are still preoccupied with inflation, even though the official rate is just 0.8pc.

“People are still worried about inflation, in particular food prices. We all discuss meat prices, they’re so high, coffee prices, chocolate prices. But that’s perceived inflation, which is higher than reality.”

Frederiksen and her centre-Left coalition partners have already started delving into the public coffers to soothe voters’ disgruntlement.

The Danish government recently cut the tax on electricity bills to almost zero. It also increased spending on childcare and welfare support and removed taxes on coffee, chocolate and sugar.

It has plenty of fiscal firepower. It was running a budget surplus estimated at more than 2pc of GDP in 2025 and its debt-to-GDP ratio was 30pc last year, one third that of Britain.

That allowed Denmark to ramp up defence spending last year by more than $4bn, which – along with higher healthcare expenditure – may prop the economy through any pharma slowdown.

The defence spending is a response to the Russia threat and also to pressure from Trump. He has called for Europe to fund its own defence and he is looking to wrestle at least de facto control over the Danish territory of Greenland.

Hansen says the Greenland issue may also have cast a pall over consumer confidence.

“Danish people are afraid that the new world order that is initiated by Mr Trump is something we have not dealt with since World War Two. That is really what is scaring the Danes,” he says.

Paradoxically, though, it may be helping the tough-talking and experienced Frederiksen. Since Trump’s Greenland manoeuvring became more explicit and tangible, her Social Democratic Party’s previously fading popularity has rebounded.

Geopolitical uncertainty and cost of living pressures are common across Europe. But in Denmark, there could be another more idiosyncratic source of economic anxiety.

Around 10pc of Danes hold shares in Novo. Many more will be exposed to its precipitous share-price decline via their private pensions.

Hansen, though, says Danish investors have been strangely patriotic: as foreigners have fled, Danes have been buying up the stock.

“The number of Danish private clients with us that hold one or more Novo Nordisk shares keeps climbing,” he says. “People still think there is a difference between what Novo Nordisk will achieve longer term and its current valuation in the stock market.”

Denmark’s economists, like its investors, also seem relatively sanguine about their country’s economic champion. Olsen says he was more worried during Novo’s breakneck growth phase than he is now.

“The fear was that it might erode the competitiveness of the rest of the economy. The large amount of hiring at Novo Nordisk could have led to very high base growth, that would then leave the rest of the economy uncompetitive if something was to go wrong,” he says.

“But what we have seen so far is that manufacturing wages growth in Denmark has, if anything, been lower over the last four or five years than we see in competitor countries. There is a competitive economy outside of Novo Nordisk.”

Pedersen agrees that the Danish economy is diversified enough to weather the Novo storm: “But of course you should keep an eye on it because the numbers are still so big on the macro level.

“Politicians do need to be aware that we have two Denmarks – one with and one without the pharmaceutical industry.”

Right now, the politician with the most riding on the Danish pharmaceuticals industry is Frederiksen.

As she called the election, she talked mostly about Greenland and wealth taxes.

Novo Nordisk’s decline might have garnered global attention with the company no longer deemed Denmark’s greatest success story.

Yet for Frederiksen, the hope is that this will simply be background noise as she bids for a third straight election victory – instead of a shot in the arm for her political rivals.

data.title: BP hails ‘exceptional’ oil trading during Iran war
data.description:
BP has hailed an “exceptional” performance at the start of the year after cashing in on surging oil prices during the Iran war.

The energy giant said it expected trading revenues to jump during the first three months of 2026 owing to “heightened volatility in crude oil, natural gas and refined products prices”.

This represents a significant rebound for BP after a weak performance in the last quarter of 2025, demonstrating how the energy shock unleashed by the closure of the Strait of Hormuz is benefitting the world’s biggest oil and gas giants.

At least a fifth of the world’s oil and gas supplies rely on the crucial trade artery.

The International Energy Agency (IEA) said global oil demand dropped sharply following the outbreak of the Iran war amid soaring prices and a shortage of supplies from the Middle East.

Figures from the IEA’s monthly oil report showed that global demand was down 3.4pc in March. It is expected to decline a further 1.1pc in April to 100.4 million barrels a day, which would be the lowest level in three years.

The IEA warned that “demand destruction will spread as scarcity and higher prices persist”.

BP highlighted the impact of surging oil and gas prices in its latest update, noting that the average price of a barrel of Brent crude hit $81 in the first quarter, up from $63 in the final three months of the year.

The oil price peaked at nearly $120 last month, although it is now hovering around $100 after the US and Iran struck a two-week ceasefire last week.

Blow to financial results

BP said the rapid changes in market conditions were “expected to impact financial results”. It added that oil production and operations would be “slightly lower compared to the fourth quarter 2025” after its operations in the Middle East were affected by the conflict.

Around a fifth of BP’s overall oil production is generated in the Middle East, although it does not operate any refineries in the region.

Other oil companies have been caught up in the conflict after their refineries were damaged by drone attacks.

The conflict represents the first challenge for Meg O’Neill, who took over as BP’s chief executive earlier this month.

She became BP’s third boss in just two and a half years following the departure of Bernard Looney after failing to disclose romantic affairs with colleagues, while her predecessor Murray Auchincloss was ousted late last year.

Ms O’Neill told staff that BP will be simplified into two main business units, moving the company back to the way it was structured before its ill-fated attempt to invest in green energy in 2020.

She said that one division of the group will focus on oil and gas production, while another will cover BP’s refining, distribution and retail activities.

In line with its current strategy, Ms O’Neill said BP would continue to redirect billions of pounds from green energy projects towards oil and gas in an attempt to increase profitability.

Shell said last week that it expected its oil trading business to report a boost in earnings following the rapid increase in oil prices.

data.title: How Sadiq Khan made London into a taxi driver’s worst nightmare
data.description:
It should be a straightforward journey that needn’t take too long – Liverpool Street to London Bridge station, two areas heavily populated with white-collar workers heading from the commuter belts into the capital for their careers.

However, those opting to drive the short one-and-a-half miles find themselves forced to go a more roundabout way – through Whitechapel, in order to avoid the bus gate at Bishopsgate, which prevents motorists (yet allows buses and bikes) from accessing the road.

So, instead of taking around five minutes in the car, it takes between 20 and 30 minutes. Realistically, it would be quicker for drivers to swap four wheels for two legs.

Bishopsgate is just one of the many roads in the capital that have become increasingly unwelcoming to motorists, with a recent survey conducted by TomTom, the satnav company, finding that London is the slowest-driving capital city in the world.

It takes an average of three minutes and 38 seconds to travel one measly kilometre (0.6 miles) in the centre of the city. The analysis also found drivers lost an average of 141 hours stuck in rush hour traffic in 2025.

This glacial pace is annoying when you are trying to get somewhere in a hurry, to attend an important meeting, perhaps, or to make it to the theatre before curtain call. However, for the black cab drivers of London, increasingly congested, slow and difficult-to-navigate roads are leading to a significant loss of earnings, and even putting their livelihoods – and the sight of the London icon – at risk of extinction.

As of late 2025, the number of licensed drivers had fallen to around 14,800, down from more than 22,500 a decade earlier.

For Steve Cox, who became a black cab driver in 2022, navigating the streets of London has become increasingly difficult. He cites low-traffic neighbourhoods (LTNs) – areas where roads are closed to traffic to allow cyclists and pedestrians to take priority – as one of the main contributors to the capital’s slow roads.

“What used to be a 15-minute journey can now take you up to an hour,” the 54-year-old explains. “Tottenham Court Road is severely restricted for motorists between 8am and 7pm, which are some of your peak hours as a black cab.

“If you want to go from one side of Tottenham Court Road to the other to drop a passenger, we have to do two different squares and go round several back roads. It’s just ridiculous.

“It’s another 15 to 20 minutes on the journey time, and probably another £15-£20 for the customer. I’m embarrassed to charge them. It’s unfair and leads to complaints that aren’t down to me – and results in fewer people trying to flag us down.”

While LTNs were initially implemented in London in the 1970s (although under a different name) to filter traffic in the Hackney area, they were implemented en masse in March 2020 under Mayor of London Sir Sadiq Khan’s plans to improve road safety, decrease air and noise pollution, and encourage “active travel” such as walking and cycling.

Now, it is thought four per cent of London’s nine-million-strong population lives in an LTN.

These have made navigating around the capital increasingly problematic; approximately 95 per cent of black cab drivers report that LTNs and poor road conditions make picking up and dropping off passengers difficult.

Cabbies also cite London’s new speed restrictions as a reason why the capital often remains at a standstill. According to Transport for London (TfL), more than half of London’s roads now have a 20mph limit. This includes all roads within London’s Congestion Charge zone, as well as major roads, such as the A107 corridor and the White Hart Roundabout.

While this was implemented to try and cut deaths and serious injuries, City Hall was previously told this change was “devastating to the taxi industry” by Steve McNamara, general secretary of the Licensed Taxi Drivers Association in 2024.

“I understand the need for 20mph zones around schools,” says Frank O’Beirne, who has been a black cab driver for 14 years. “However, places like Park Lane, which have been reduced from three lanes of traffic to just one in order for there to be a bus and cycling lane, is just madness – especially now we’re stuck only being able to go 20mph there.”

The 50-year-old adds: “While our meters do go up in traffic, it’s only incremental – we need to be moving to actually have decent fares. Customers get so fed up in traffic they just jump out.”

Sadiq Khan’s green crusade for London has reached a nadir with the highly criticised and controversial ultra-low emission zone (Ulez). Since 2018, all newly-licenced black cabs need to be “zero-emission capable”, while the maximum operating age for older, more polluting diesel taxis has been reduced from 15 years to 12.

Cox says some older cabbies have been forced into an early retirement. “I know men in their 60s who have had their cab for 10 years and just didn’t see the point of carrying on investing so much money in a new car, and have gone and got jobs in Tesco instead.

“That and the stress of trying to earn enough money to keep you afloat is enough to push someone into another job. I don’t blame people for saying: ‘I don’t need this anymore.’”

It’s of little surprise, then, that a quarter of all black cabs on the roads before the pandemic are no longer in operation. Renting a traditional black cab, or Hackney carriage, is not cheap. Cox pays £360 a week to rent his vehicle, while others pay £400 and beyond. That doesn’t include the additional outgoings for cabbies, such as the mandatory two MOTs a year; while cab drivers don’t pay this, it does result in a loss of earnings as you can’t work while your vehicle is being serviced.

Cox says he needs to earn more than £3,000 a month just to break even on the investments he’s made to become a black cab driver. “It’s a stressful trade,” he says. “It’s made even more stressful by sudden roadworks, or ongoing protests by groups such as Palestine Action blocking roads. I can lose up to £300 a day in earnings when protests take place.”

Those still working as black cab drivers are now finding alternative methods to keep themselves afloat. O’Beirne works as a tour guide during slow periods. “I prefer doing out-of-town stuff now, because London just drives me mad,” he says. “Places like Tottenham Court Road, Mayfair, the City are just no-go areas. It’s a nightmare to navigate.

“Corporate gigs tend to pay better – I get a lot of American customers, who tend to be big tippers. But doing these gigs means you give up some freedom of setting your own hours, which is partly the beauty of doing this job.”

So, what could help London’s black cab drivers stay on the roads? For Cox, cutting, or at least reducing, some of the restrictions of LTNs on Hackney carriages would help with picking up and dropping off customers.

“If we really want to ease congestion, we could put a cap on private-hire drivers,” he adds. “They can operate without any restriction on the size of their fleet. I’ve heard of private-hire drivers getting licensed in Wolverhampton and then driving down here to get more work.”

Currently, private-hire vehicles vastly outstrip black cabs; TfL estimates more than 106,000 of these cabs are on the road.

Meanwhile, Steve Kenton, who has been a cabbie for 35 years, believes City Hall should listen more to black cab drivers. “We have some wonderful and intelligent people who choose to become black cab drivers,” he says. “We spend three to four years perfecting the Knowledge of London so we know the proper routes around the city. We attract individuals from every demographic. Our views and concerns deserve to be treated with more respect.”

“Fundamentally, if our wheels aren’t moving, we aren’t making money,” O’Beirne agrees. “We’re the only fully accessible transport network in London. Not everyone can ride a bike.”

data.title: Labour blames OpenAI’s cash struggles for data centre cancellation
data.description:
A Labour minister has claimed OpenAI’s “challenging” financial situation is behind the decision to halt data centre investments in the UK, rejecting claims that high energy costs are to blame.

Kanishka Narayan, the AI minister, said that the ChatGPT developer dropped out of the project, called Stargate UK, because of its “internal financing context”.

This marks a split with OpenAI, which singled out the cost of energy when pulling the plug on an AI data centre in the North East last week.

The AI lab said it would revisit the plans, announced in September, when “the right conditions, such as regulation and the cost of energy, enable long-term infrastructure investment”.

However, Mr Narayan has now appeared to reject OpenAI’s comments.

Speaking on The Rest is Money podcast, he said there had been “no change to energy pricing, and there has been no change to the regulatory environment”.

“What has changed is, as is well publicised, OpenAI’s internal financing context has become more challenging and they have made similar calls in the United States with their programme there as well,” he said.

Britain has the highest industrial electricity prices in the developed world, although UK energy costs have fallen slightly since OpenAI unveiled plans for Stargate UK.

Data centres, and particularly AI systems, are energy-intensive, making them vulnerable to high electricity costs.

OpenAI confirmed this month that it has raised $122bn (£97bn) in new funding and the business is believed to be targeting a $1tn public float as soon as this year.

But it also burned through an estimated $14bn in 2025. This has led the company to cut back on costly projects, such as scrapping Sora, its expensive AI video app.

OpenAI, which is valued at $830bn, revealed its $500bn Stargate data centre investment plan at a White House press conference in January last year.

The project has since been beset by delays, while OpenAI has retreated from some of its other data centre ventures.

In Norway, OpenAI pulled out of another data project this week. It confirmed it would no longer rent AI computing power directly from the 100pc clean energy data site and instead lease capacity from Microsoft.

Since December, OpenAI’s international expansion efforts have been led by George Osborne, the former Conservative chancellor.

In the US, OpenAI also cancelled an expansion plan for its primary data facility in Abilene, Texas.

OpenAI declined to comment.
