data.title: The collapse of Starmer’s housebuilding revolution
data.description:
For Weston Homes, a mid-sized property developer in London’s commuter belt, Sir Keir Starmer’s election victory was supposed to be a godsend.

The Labour leader had promised to “bulldoze” England’s planning system and unlock a housebuilding revolution, staking his reputation on building an unprecedented 1.5 million homes by 2030.

But almost two years on, something has gone badly wrong. Before Sir Keir was elected, Weston Homes was building 1,000 new homes per year. This has now plunged to 600.

“Our output was probably last that low at the back end of the global financial crisis,” said Bob Weston, the company’s chairman.

“The UK might not be in a technical economic recession, but we are, without question, in a housing recession.

“Everybody is scaling back. Construction industry jobs are falling like flies. Pretty much every construction site in the UK is on go-slow.”

Weston says the outlook for housebuilders is the worst he has seen in his 50-year career.

At a national level, the data tells an alarming tale.

Despite Labour’s promises, the number of new homes built last year fell to a decade-low.

Even more worryingly, the number of homes securing planning consent tumbled by 27pc to levels last seen in the early 2010s.

Campaign group Britain Remade estimates that, based on the current pipeline and average build times, England is on track to deliver just 706,000 new homes over this parliament.

This represents just 47pc of Labour’s target and 5pc less than the number of new homes delivered under the previous government.

In London, analysts expect the lowest annual building rates in the post-Second World War era.

A range of problems is underlying this latest crunch, including time.

Although Labour has made a series of structural planning reforms widely commended by industry, including scope to unlock swathes of land, it will be years before the benefits are realised.

It is also the sad reality that planning was only ever part of the problem.

Elsewhere, developers are burdened by red tape and too many people still cannot afford to buy new-build homes.

The war in Iran has also now unleashed more turmoil, as soaring energy prices and elevated borrowing costs are bound to increase construction costs and dent demand.

Ultimately, it appears Sir Keir’s housebuilding pledge is dead in the water.

Housing boom on paper only

To its credit, Labour acted swiftly to tackle the planning system.

Within days of the election in July 2024, Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, announced that mandatory housing targets would be reimposed on local authorities.

By the end of the month, Labour had published a draft national planning policy framework (NPPF) that increased the number of homes councils were expected to approve.

It also created a “grey belt” designation to unlock development on protected green belt land. Data company LandTech estimated that this could open up space for up to four million new homes.

A new draft NPPF published last December also outlined plans to encourage development in the “train belt”.

The idea being that if developers submit plans to build projects of a certain density within 800 metres of major railway stations, they should be approved by default. Lichfields planning consultants expect that this will unlock land to develop 633,000 homes.

This radical policy shift has been effective.

Local authorities are allocating sites for housing quicker, while also prompting conversations with housebuilders about increasing development, says Mark Adams, the executive chairman of Pennyfarthing, a housebuilder in the South of England.

Because planning applications are now much more likely to get waved through, Britain’s builders are also submitting more of them.

A senior source at a major housebuilder says their firm has doubled planning applications made on sites in their long-term land portfolio as a result of these reforms.

“Two years ago, we were progressing with planning applications for about 33pc of our portfolio,” they said. “Now it’s about 66pc. So that’s a 100pc increase.

“In the quarter of a century I’ve been doing this, there’s been nothing like as positive a planning system as we’ve got now.”

But this does not mean more homes are actually getting built, because council planners remain woefully under-resourced. That is alongside frequent opposition from local Nimbys (not in my back yard).

This has sparked a planning backlog as applications for major sites are taking two or three years to be approved.

The number of homes gaining full planning permission fell in every single quarter last year, according to Savills.

Across 2025, just 180,000 properties were approved for development. Not only was this the lowest level in more than 20 years, but it was also down by a quarter from the year before Labour won the election.

In London, the number of homes getting planning consent more than halved year on year.

Stuck in the doldrums

According to Simon Coop, of Lichfields planning consultants, there has been no real change in the way that local authorities approach planning decisions.

“Nearly two years into the parliamentary term, you’re getting the sense that nothing’s changing,” he says.

Ironically, the new surge in planning applications may have made things worse, says Greg Hill, of The Hill Group, a housebuilder in London and the South.

“The system remains very challenging, very slow,” he says. “Because there are more applications in the system, it puts more pressure on the system. So there’s planning activity but not housing delivery activity.”

In 2024, Labour announced it would employ 300 new planning officers, but this has so far made little difference.

This week, the British Property Federation (BPF) warned that it will take an additional 3,000 officers to give local authorities the manpower needed to manage their workloads.

Low levels of planning approvals mean just 3.4pc of the population live in local authorities which are on track to meet their new housing targets, according to Britain Remade.

Savills’ analysis shows that in London, which has a housing target of 88,000 per year, the number of homes that secured planning consent last year was little more than 20,000.

The national pipeline is also dismal. In 2025, just 202,800 new homes were completed, the lowest total in any calendar year since 2015, including the pandemic.

This was down 3pc year on year, 18pc less than in 2019 and nearly a third short of the Government’s annual target of building 300,000 homes per year.

Starts on new homes has climbed since Labour took office, but they are still in the doldrums.

Office for National Statistics (ONS) data show that work started on 118,000 new homes in England in the 12 months to September.

Red tape burden

This was up by 30pc compared to the 12 months before the 2024 election. But excluding that period, it was the lowest 12-month total since 2013.

James Moody, at London property developer Seven Capital, says: “Their manifesto pledge has failed. That is the honest answer.”

Even Steve Reed, the Housing Secretary, acknowledged the crisis last month by warning that a lack of new homes meant young people were living with their parents into their 40s.

“It’s not acceptable,” he said. “The opportunities that were open to my generation, when we were younger, are closed off to them.”

One key factor Sir Keir seems to have overlooked is that planning is only part of the problem.

While Labour has done a good job of reducing planning hurdles, it has not addressed the regulatory burdens on construction, which are growing, says Sam Dumitriu, at Britain Remade, an economic think tank.

For decades, housebuilders have had to make large financial contributions to the local areas where they are building.

From 2010, councils have been able to charge developers for new infrastructure under the Community Infrastructure Levy (CIL).

This came on top of pre-existing requirements for housebuilders to make contributions to affordable housing under Section 106 of the Town and Country Planning Act.

When Sir Sadiq Khan was elected Mayor of London in 2016, he set a requirement that 35pc of new housing developments should be “affordable” homes, which are sold at below market rates.

The 2017 Grenfell fire, which killed 72 people, triggered a new wave of building safety regulations.

From April 2022, large builders have had to pay a Residential Property Developer Tax (RPDT) to fund the country’s cladding remediation bill.

In the same year, requirements were introduced for the new Building Safety Regulator to approve plans for high-rise developments.

This process takes an average of nine months to give sign-off and has so far seen 69pc of pre-construction applications get rejected, according to Britain Remade.

From October 2026, developers will also have to pay the building safety levy, another tax to pay for cladding remediation.

At the same time, environmental rules have tightened dramatically.

Self-storage ‘more valuable’

In 2019, the Government introduced “nutrient neutrality” rules that mean housing developments must not increase soil pollutants in protected areas.

The Future Homes Standard, which will come into effect in March 2027, will also require new homes to be fitted with low-carbon forms of heating.

“There isn’t anyone who does what we do who hasn’t got a long list of examples of sites where they’re held up due to red tape, bureaucracy, regulations and other items,” says Hill. “We have a litany of examples.”

Mandatory timelines for decision-making are rarely adhered to, he adds. “We’re perpetually in delay on everything we do.”

A new report by the Home Builders Federation (HBF) being published next week is expected to show that in the last five years, the cost of building a new home has increased by more than £70,000 because of new taxes, regulations and build cost inflation.

Zoopla estimated last year that half of the country was no longer viable to develop.

At Seven Capital’s 100 Kensington development, a 462-home project under construction in west London, the company is paying nearly £70m in developer taxes and community contributions at the site, says Moody.

This is about a third of the project’s total build costs, which are roughly double what they would have been a decade ago, he says.

“The planning tax is about £60m more than we will make, and that is before I pay tax on any profits,” says Moody.

“I’ve been doing this for 30 years and I’ve been through a number of recessions. I think this is the most difficult set of circumstances I’ve ever operated in. Because we live in an environment full of regulators, not facilitators.”

Seven Capital has another 178-home development in Islington, which has been in the planning process since 2021.

Since Seven Capital logged the planning application, inflation and new regulations have increased the cost of building the site by about 30pc, says Moody. Sale prices, however, have not moved.

“It is probably more valuable as a self-storage site than a housing site.”

Jon Stoddart, co-head of planning at CBRE, says: “It’s just a very heavily taxed industry. Whilst the Government has had some really good policy initiatives, there still seems to be a fundamental issue around the viability of development.”

London falling

These issues are most acute in London – partly because the capital has a higher density of tall buildings subject to new building safety regulations, but also because there are not enough buyers who can afford to pay for what the builders are selling.

The average home in the capital costs 10.6 times a typical person’s earnings. Technically, this ratio has eased from its 2021 peak of 12.9, but because interest rates have climbed since Covid, mortgage costs are actually far higher.

Based on current rates, the monthly mortgage bill for a buyer purchasing an average London home with a 10pc deposit is now £3,000, up from £2,000 in 2021. Zoopla calculated last year that a buyer needs to earn more than £100,000 per year to afford to buy a property in the capital.

“The biggest thing for housebuilding at the moment, aside from the planning, is demand,” a source at a major housebuilder says. “People need to be able to afford to buy. That side of the coin has been neglected.”

Squeezed affordability matters even more for developers now because of the loss of the Help to Buy equity loan scheme, which ran from 2013 until 2023.

Nationally, this supported around 40,000 new build sales per year, by providing first-time buyers purchasing new homes with 20pc government-backed equity loans that were interest-free for the first five years.

“First-time buyers are in the worst situation they’ve been in for decades,” says Weston.

Before the pandemic, first-time buyers made up 70pc of Weston Homes’ buyers. Now, they purchase only 30pc of the homes that Weston sells.

The Help-to-Buy crutch was removed at the same time that the Government was pushing a buy-to-let tax crackdown, which has eroded the business case for private rental investment in the capital.

Changes to anti-money laundering rules, falling prices and a new stamp duty surcharge for overseas buyers in 2021 have also hit demand among foreign investors.

New-build developers were heavily reliant on these two buyer groups to purchase new homes off plan, which was a vital source of cash flow during development, says James Barton, joint head of London Land Agency at Knight Frank. Now these buyers have dried up.

“Developers can deal with two or three headwinds, but at the moment they have a perfect storm of eight or nine issues that they can’t control,” says Barton.

London is now at the epicentre of Britain’s housebuilding drought. Private housing starts in London fell by half in 2025 to just 5,500, according to data company Molior. This was 83pc less than in 2015.

Based on the capital’s current pipeline, Molior expects just 14,053 homes will be completed across 2027 and 2028, just 8pc of the Government’s two-year London housing target of 176,000.

This would be the first time since 1946 that London has completed no more than 10,000 homes a year.

And what is in the pipeline is being put on hold. There are currently 893 planning permissions for unbuilt schemes in London, according to Molior. In the last 12 months, construction started on just 73 of these.

“More than 90pc of permissions just aren’t going anywhere,” says Tim Craine, Molior’s founder.

Ben Hopkinson, at the Centre for Policy Studies, says: “It’s very, very bad. London’s facing its strongest headwinds for housebuilding since the Second World War.

“Starts are down that much that, if something drastic doesn’t happen, we’re set for record low levels of housebuilding.”

In October, Sir Sadiq and Reed announced a package of emergency measures to boost London housebuilding, which included reducing the affordable homes requirement from 35pc to 20pc. However, analysts warn it is not enough to shift the dial.

At the start of April, London-focused housebuilder Berkeley Group said it was halting land purchases because of “unprecedented” increases in costs and regulation.

“We have ended up in a situation where the place where we need to build homes the most has become the place where it’s hardest to build homes. It’s a real disaster,” says Dumitriu.

Impact of war in Iran

Now, the war in Iran has made a bad situation even worse.

“Seven weeks ago, things were genuinely improving,” says an industry source. “Mortgage affordability was improving and you were seeing an improvement in sales rates. Then Iran happened.”

Iran’s blockade of the Strait of Hormuz has sent oil prices soaring past $100 per barrel, reviving inflation fears and driving up government borrowing costs. Mortgage rates have jumped as traders price in the prospect of Bank of England interest rate rises.

The average rate on a two-year fixed-rate mortgage has climbed from 4.83pc at the start of March to 5.89pc, according to Moneyfacts. For a buyer taking out a typical £200,000 home loan, this has added £3,024 in interest payments to the cost of the loan over two years.

The blow to buyer demand is significant. Adams, of Pennyfarthing, says the number of buyers visiting the company’s new-build sites has plunged by 50pc since the war began.

“There’s this lack of confidence because of what’s going on in the UK and the wider world,” says Adams. “People can see where mortgage rates are going and they are looking over their shoulder at energy prices and the general cost of living.”

Pennyfarthing has contractors lined up at a site in the South of England, which has planning permission for 340 homes.

However, it has now put the development on hold, in large part resulting from fears over falling demand. “We’ve had it on a rolling review for the last four weeks,” says Adams.

The other big looming threat is rising construction costs, a source at a housebuilder warns: “The longer the war goes on, the worse the impact gets.”

UK construction costs in February were already 42pc higher than in January 2020, after the pandemic supply chain crisis and inflation wave.

Soon, higher oil prices will flow through to higher costs of bricks, steel, concrete and other energy-intensive construction products.

Housebuilders face a “perfect storm” in the second half of this year as they brace for a double whammy of elevated costs and lower demand, warns Noble Francis, chief economist at the Construction Products Association (CPA).

Consequently, the listed UK housebuilder stocks have tanked. Barratt Redrow, Berkeley Group, Vistry and Taylor Wimpey have dropped by 32pc, 14pc, 47pc and 21pc respectively so far this year.

Whitehall sources have also been warning that the war jeopardises the Government’s target to build 1.5 million homes.

For Sir Keir, failing to deliver on housebuilding means not only an acute shortage of homes but also the death of Labour’s promise to secure the highest sustained growth rate in the G7.

New housing creates growth partly through construction sector jobs and profits, but also by reducing rents and enabling the workforce to be able to move to where the best jobs are, which boosts productivity.

“If Labour don’t have that growth story from housing, then where is the growth going to come from?” says Dumitriu.

“A failure to get a grip on this housing issue by the end of this parliament means they’re going to be in real trouble.”

A spokesman for the Housing Department said: “We will build the homes this country needs.

“We’ve overhauled the planning system and invested a record £39bn in social and affordable housing to help developers get spades in the ground and weather any geopolitical pressure.

“This is alongside emergency measures in London to unblock dozens of stalled sites and build thousands more homes.”

data.title: Tesco cannot guarantee shoppers will be shielded from food shortages
data.description:
The boss of Tesco has said he cannot guarantee that shoppers will not experience food shortages this summer.

Ken Murphy, the chief executive of Britain’s biggest supermarket, said it “would be wrong” for him to guarantee that customers will not see supply issues as a result of the situation in the Middle East.

“Can I provide guarantees? Of course, not,” he told The Telegraph.

“Given how quickly the situation globally changes and the volatility of the actors involved in this conflict, I think [it] would be wrong for me to give you any guarantee.

“I’m giving you a commitment that Tesco will do its very best to minimise the impact on customers, and I think that we’ve demonstrated that commitment over the last five years.”

Mr Murphy was speaking after it emerged the Government has drawn up contingency plans for potential food shortages if the war in Iran continued.

The Times revealed that officials have conducted an analysis for a “reasonable worst-case scenario” where carbon dioxide would be in short supply, which could result in shortages of chicken and pork.

The analysis is based on the Strait of Hormuz not being reopened by summer and no permanent peace deal.

Peter Kyle, the Business Secretary, confirmed planning for possible food shortages was under way on Thursday and said Sir Keir Starmer was “leading from the front”.

Mr Kyle told Times Radio: “Of course, we don’t comment on leaks. But this is now out there. But I want to reassure people that we have been acting since the very start of this conflict.”

He added: “We are looking at a variety of scenarios... When people read what is a leak, and it is difficult for me because these leaks are very unhelpful, but when people read it, they need to be reassured that we are doing this planning and we are doing this kind of scenario planning.

“I can tell you because I’m in these meetings that the Prime Minister has been there since the start of this war and he is going through and driving deep dives into resilience in lots of areas in our economy. You have a Government that is acting with creativity and boldness.”

Mr Kyle insisted food shortages were currently “not a concern” and said he would be “upfront” with the public if that were to change.

Mr Murphy said the Government was doing “the right thing” in drawing up contingency plans, adding: “I think the Government is planning and scenario-managing worst-case scenarios, and then saying, how would we cope in that scenario?”

He said Tesco was not currently experiencing any supply issues, with none of its farmers or growers having flagged problems.

Mr Murphy said: “We are constantly working on various scenarios ourselves internally, as you would expect us to do. None of our suppliers have flagged any issues to us at this point. I think that’s an important point.”

He said he welcomed the Government decision last month to reopen a Teesside carbon dioxide production facility, saying this was “really good news”.

The National Pig Association, which represents farmers and processors of the animals, said it was “concerned” about the impact of the war in the Middle East.

“If the production and therefore availability of CO2 is restricted, we would expect this to impact supply,” a spokesman said, calling for the meat industry to be prioritised for supply of the gas.

data.title: Homebuyers and bereaved lose £40m from law firm fraud
data.description:
Suspected fraud at a collapsed UK law firm has left homeowners and bereaved families facing losses of £39.5m, according to the legal watchdog.

The Solicitors Regulation Authority (SRA) confirmed an investigation into suspected fraud at PM Law because of claims regarding the “improper removal and misuse” of client funds.

That includes theft of housing deposits and money left behind in wills.

It is not yet clear who is behind the fraud, as the SRA said the investigation is “one of the largest and most complex” it has ever undertaken. The SRA said it is working to compensate the firm’s former clients.

It comes after PM Law collapsed on Feb 2 earlier this year, creating uncertainty for thousands of clients and putting hundreds of employees out of work.

Former staff at PM Law told The Telegraph that the law firm’s sudden collapse came as a shock, with some employees only finding out from notices posted on their office doors.

One former staff member said they initially thought the office was shut because of renovation work before being informed by their manager that the entire business had closed down.

Another ex-employee described PM Law’s collapse as a “horrendous situation for everyone involved”.

The SRA initially intervened two days after the closure of PM Law, before seizing tens of thousands of client files. PM Law entered voluntary liquidation on March 3.

Millions already returned to clients

The watchdog said it has started processing £21.5m of claims submitted by PM Law’s former clients.

This has led to £9.3m being returned to 92 former clients, although it predicts an additional £18m in outstanding claims that haven’t been submitted.

Paul Hastings, the SRA’s director of client protection, said PM Law’s collapse had caused “significant upheaval” for many former clients.

That includes some who were in the process of buying or selling a home and faced losing their deposits if the purchases collapsed.

“Others were dealing with delayed probate matters, often while managing bereavement and outstanding estate administration,” he said.

PM Law traces its origins to a firm called Proddow Mackay, which was founded in Maidenhead, Berkshire, in 1990 by Donald Mackay, a solicitor who owned a sizeable stake in the business until its closure.

The firm later grew into a larger network through a series of acquisitions of high-street law firms.

At the time of its collapse, the Sheffield business owned 11 high-street law firms across Britain, including Butterworths Solicitors, Autonet Law and Proddow Mackay, mainly specialising in house sales, wills and personal injury claims.

The firm was headquartered in an office building called PM House on the outskirts of Sheffield, but ran 25 separate offices in Yorkshire, Cumbria, Berkshire, Derbyshire and London that employed more than 600 people.

Administrators for PM Law were contacted for comment.

data.title: London is becoming a Potemkin city
data.description:
What is happening to our cities? Physically, they are being hollowed out, as old buildings are eviscerated: their hinderparts completely destroyed and replaced, leaving only a thin veneer of original façade to the street. What a metaphor for a society that, in the political and money-making departments, seems devoid of principle.

Seeming survivals of old London, Manchester or Glasgow are often in reality no such thing. A fig leaf covers an obscenity of enormous proportions. Peek behind the frontage when the site is under construction and you will see a Goldfinger’s lair, the urban equivalent of the arch villain’s Kentucky stud farm in the 1964 film. Not only will the whole city block have been cleared but ground will have been dug out to sink many levels of basement into the bowels of the earth.

Goldfinger himself – I refer to the Modernist architect Ernő Goldfinger, not his fictional namesake – would have been horrified. Honesty to structure was a sacred tenet to followers of the so-called International Style, which arrived from the Continent in the 1930s. Applied decoration was anathema.

Propagandists for the style borrowed the language of A.W.N. Pugin, the Victorian architect. He had put welly into the Gothic Revival by making it a moral cause. Pointed (i.e. medieval) architecture was structurally consistent, Christian and pure; places such as Ramsgate, where, being a contrarian, he had chosen to live, were debased by stucco that hid shoddy brickwork and was painted to look like stone.

After the Second World War, the tenets of Modernism went unchallenged. However much ordinary people were attached to terraced streets, the only morally valid building technology was that which used steel, concrete and glass. The march of progress had ordained it. Pastiche was verboten.

Although the collapse of a new 22-storey tower called Ronan Point following a gas explosion in 1968 revealed (literal) cracks in the premise, the mood among developers and planners did not change until 1984, when the then Prince Charles articulated what many of the public were already saying to each other in his famous Carbuncle speech. The Prince had read David Watkin’s Morality and Architecture, published in 1977, which showed that the intellectual foundations of Modernism were piffle.

This created a problem. If Modernism fell foul of conservationists, whose voice was now being heard, how were developers to provide the sort of office buildings their clients wanted, whilst also making oodles of money? They couldn’t simply flatten pre-existing streets as they had done before.

The answer? Façadism. Keep the fronts and fool the public into believing that Dickens or Boswell might have strolled past and found nothing amiss, while building a completely new frame at the back. This became established practice wherever historic building considerations raised their heads. Conservation areas have been, in many cases, turned into shells as a consequence.

It is – no surprise – all driven by money, not only the desire to avoid costly delays in the planning process but the iniquitous Treasury policy of charging VAT on works to old buildings while giving a free pass to new construction. It works like this: developers propose to knock down a listed building; general horror erupts; they then accept a compromise which involves keeping the façade while pocketing the VAT exemption because, after all, the result will be 99 per cent new.

This scam offends as the holiest of 21st-century objectives: the reduction of carbon emissions. Old buildings lock up large quantities of embedded carbon, whereas new ones are massive emitters; so it’s better to work with old structures as far as possible.

I will admit that façadism does have one small virtue: it keeps the street line intact. How grateful we were then, as Britain emerged blinking from the Modernist hegemony, for that small saving grace. Streets are essential for happiness. They are where people meet each other. Visitors can instantly “read” them, seeing where the shops are, finding the front doors.

Stand-alone Modernist buildings were surrounded by soulless piazzas, where rubbish drifted like tumbleweed. Now, incredibly, numerous architects are working with the street, not against it. Not just traditionalists such as ADAM Architecture and Ben Pentreath, in the Charles III camp, but young firms such as Peter Barber Architects and Trevor Morriss of SPPARC.

On an unintended drive around South London earlier this week, after being misdirected by sat nav, I saw many examples. Come on guys, we can afford more self-belief. Keep old buildings intact, build new ones that make decent streets. Our cities deserve it.

data.title: Major housebuilder almost halves in value as Iran war hits profits
data.description:
One of Britain’s biggest housebuilders has almost halved in value after bosses unveiled contingency measures to avoid a debt crunch.

Crest Nicholson told investors on Tuesday that it had kickstarted emergency talks with lenders to secure “temporary” relief on its banking covenants.

The announcement, which also included a profit warning the housebuilder blamed on war in Iran, led to more than £124m being wiped off its value as shares fell by as much as 45pc.

Martyn Clark, the chief executive, said Crest had been hit by the conflict in the Middle East, fuelling the prospect of a “prolonged higher interest rate environment, renewed cost pressures and a deterioration in consumer confidence”.

This is likely to hamper sales for Crest, which now expects to generate earnings before interest and tax of just £5m to £15m for the full year, dramatically below January’s guidance of at least £32m.

As a result it is seeking relief on its debts.

The builder has cut its yearly sales guidance to between 1,400 and 1,500 homes, down from as many as 1,700. Meanwhile, it expects net debt to reach up to £120m, revised up from £15m-£65m.

It also warned of mounting construction costs triggered by soaring energy prices.

Bricks, blocks and steel are among the construction materials that are heavily reliant on energy for production.

Crest Nicholson is the latest in a string of housebuilders facing mounting financial pressures caused by the Gulf conflict, as higher inflation is likely to increase borrowing costs for buyers, hurting demand.

Berkeley Group also recently sounded the alarm by halting new land purchases and scaling back investment in its building projects.

Crest warned that new enquiries had begun to fall, noting that the South has seen the sharpest drop in activity.

Separately, the company cut its land sales revenue forecast to around £40m, less than the £75m to £100m it had targeted, and said it no longer expected to make “material” profit from selling land before its financial year ends in October.

Mr Clark said the housebuilder was moving “quickly” to shore up its balance sheet, insisting that it was well-placed to weather the uncertainty.

“We are doing what needs to be done to navigate this uncertainty to best position the business to deliver the attractive medium-term opportunity,” he said.​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

data.title: Supermarkets ‘taking the mickey’ on olive oil prices, says Filippo Berio
data.description:
The boss of Filippo Berio has accused supermarkets of “taking the mickey” out of customers with their sky-high olive oil prices.

Walter Zanre told Sky News that wholesale olive oil prices had fallen in recent months but said supermarkets were refusing to follow suit.

“We brought prices down twice last year and it’s not all been passed on to the consumer, which is a huge frustration,” he said.

It follows a surge in the price of olive oil since 2022, after adverse weather in olive-growing countries led to poor harvests.

Spain, which produces almost half of the world’s olive oil, was hit by droughts and heatwaves in 2023 and 2024. The weather caused global olive oil production to fall by almost one million tonnes.

It caused higher prices for many well-known olive oils, including Filippo Berio, which uses a blend of oils from across Europe despite being an Italian brand.

The average price of a 500ml bottle of Filippo Berio has risen from £3.75 in 2022 to £7.50 now, according to the tracker Trolley.co.uk.

Tesco’s own-brand olive oil stands at £5.50 for 500ml. This is up from £5.20 this time last year, Trolley.co.uk data shows. The same-size bottle of Nicolas Alziari olive oil, at Waitrose, sells for £22.50.

The boss of Filippo Berio argued that prices should have come down rapidly for customers after stronger production more recently meant there was better availability of oil.

According to the International Council of Olive Growers, the wholesale Italian olive oil price was down 30pc in March compared to last year’s figures.

‘Expand their margins’

Mr Zanre, who has worked for the olive oil brand for more than 25 years, said producers had reduced the price at which they sell to supermarkets, but that this was not being seen on the shelves.

He said: “We can’t dictate retail prices. For me, it’s immensely frustrating that supermarkets have taken the opportunity to expand their margins.”

Mr Zanre accused supermarkets of profiteering from strong demand for olive oil, suggesting grocery bosses had probably been surprised at how resilient olive oil sales had been in recent years, even though it was more expensive, and so had decided not to drop prices.

His claims prompted a backlash from retail leaders on Tuesday, who argued that they passed on reductions where they could.

Andrew Opie, the director of food and sustainability at the British Retail Consortium, said: “Retailers work hard to pass on cost savings to customers wherever possible and, as confirmed by the Competition and Markets Authority, operate on very tight margins, reflecting a market driven by savvy customers.

“Olive oil, like many everyday products, is something shoppers can compare across brands and retailers to take advantage of promotions or switch to alternatives that suit their budget.”

The attack against supermarkets comes amid fears that prices for olive oil could soon start rising again.

The war in Iran, and consequent fuel crisis from the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, is expected to hit olive growers because they need diesel for their tractors as well as to fuel water pumps which irrigate the olive groves. It means production costs will probably rise this season.

It will add to pain for shoppers, who have been told to brace for higher prices across supermarket shelves. The Food & Drink Federation warned earlier this month that food inflation could hit 10pc by the end of the year, up from early forecasts of 3.2pc.

It said the war was having a “direct and immediate impact” on costs for UK food and drink manufacturers.

data.title: Net zero zealots are putting Gen Z off building planes
data.description:
Britain’s aerospace companies are gearing up for their biggest boom in a generation.

Record airliner orders will require plane makers and their suppliers to lift production by a fifth in the next few years, creating well-paid jobs for thousands of workers.

Yet a UK aerospace industry that produced the jet engine, the Harrier jump jet and Concorde is being hampered by a growing recruitment crisis.

Years of demonisation by green campaigners such as Greta Thunberg for its inability to wean itself off fossil fuels are beginning to take their toll as young people turn away from careers in industry.

A lack of inspirational new aircraft and the allure of rival jobs in technology and AI are compounding the situation, leaving even the biggest firms struggling to hire.

Airbus, which employs 15,000 people in the UK, is resorting to converting one of its Beluga wing transporters into a recruitment centre in an effort to bolster its appeal to Gen Z.

The firm will fit the plane with classrooms and a mock-up of its assembly line as it seeks to recruit hundreds of apprentices a year to the Broughton wing plant in North Wales.

Bosses are hoping that Nasa’s Artemis programme to return humans to the Moon by 2028 will also help restore some of the wider industry’s lost glamour.

Richard Gardner, the honorary president of Farnborough Air Sciences Trust, said Gen Z had been turned off aviation by eco-zealots who wrongly condemn the sector for its environmental impact.

He suggested the problem starts in school, where children are increasingly taught about the impacts of climate change.

He said: “We talk about the word brainwashing, which is quite emotive for some people, but that is what it is.

“The teaching unions are very influential in terms of the way that profession is going, and they are pretty green in their tendencies.”

Gardner added: “I think they mostly buy into these green policies, science and economics teachers aside, and that aerospace and defence are therefore not high in their career recommendations.”

He said the recent rise of the Green Party as an electoral force is also a “big worry” for aviation.

Green policies include a commitment to banning domestic flights for journeys that could be made by train, halting airport expansion and imposing a tax on frequent travellers.

Kevin Craven, the chief executive of ADS, the trade group for aerospace, defence and space firms, said that while the industry might not be decarbonising fast enough for some young people, its focus on reducing emissions may be failing to motivate others.

He said: “There is a feeling that aerospace has faded into the background a bit because there are no longer any mountains being climbed.

“Concorde, the Comet, the jet engine and the Harrier were all British firsts that really excited and inspired people.”

Craven added: “All new aircraft are really a cylinder with wings and look approximately the same. There are incremental advances which are huge for sustainability, but it’s hard to see that progression.

“There is also huge competition from other high-tech arenas – particularly AI – and, more generally, the City, where vast swathes of money are available to the best and brightest.”

Mr Craven said it was galling to see tech companies recruiting the most able engineers, even though their electricity consumption is set to double by 2030, creating clear parallels with the net zero challenges facing aviation.

He said: “When you look at cloud computing, on the other side of it is a dirty big data centre requiring huge amounts of power and huge amounts of cooling.”

‘Incredibly competitive world’

Sue Partridge, the UK country manager for Airbus, said the world’s biggest plane maker had turned to initiatives such as the Beluga recruitment project as it prepares to deliver 1,000 jet planes a year as early as 2027, up from 793 in 2025.

Airbus’s Filton, Gloucestershire, wing design and development centre near Bristol faces competition for A-level students and graduate recruits from a broad spectrum of rival employers, she said.

The firm, which has trained 4,000 British recruits in the past decade, also faces a fight to hire 16-year-old apprentices for its assembly line in Broughton, Flintshire, with Merseyside’s carmakers and the cluster of green energy firms on nearby Deeside, Wales.

She said: “It’s an incredibly competitive world out there, and we need to do everything we can to attract talent and secure those skills we are going to need for the future.

“But it is difficult, particularly in newer digital skill areas, where we are competing with Google and companies like that, which was never the case in the past.”

Partridge added: “With the younger cohort, the challenge is attracting them to the industry in the first place and inspiring them so that they want to actually build aeroplanes. They get influenced in their career choice at an early age.

“A few years ago, all the stories were about flight shaming, and aerospace was the bad guy. That’s why we want to get into schools so they really connect with Airbus and aerospace manufacturing.”

The Beluga, named after the white whale whose bloated fuselage it resembles, garners rare attention for a modern plane and would be a formidable tool in encouraging take-up of Stem (science, technology, engineering and mathematics) subjects, Ms Partridge said.

“There are still inspirational stories we can tell, and I think the Beluga is one,” she said. “We know people love the plane, and we can use that as a symbol.

“It’s an iconic aircraft, and if you see one in the sky, for me, it’s just as impactful as Concorde used to be. We already have a Concorde near Filton, South Gloucestershire, and we can use this in the same way.”

Mr Craven said the development of so-called flying taxis, in which Britain is playing a leading role, is one area that should resonate with younger people.

That is largely “because they will be silent, and there won’t be obvious pollutants pouring out of the back”.

However, it is not clear how big the market for the electric vertical take-off craft will be, with some industry observers concerned that they will be limited to whisking the very rich to and from first-class flights, a role hardly likely to endear them to Gen Z.

Industry experts hope that a new era of manned space travel could achieve greater cut-through.

Elon Musk’s SpaceX has made space a “much closer frontier”, while the Artemis missions have the potential to capture hearts and minds just as the Apollo programme did in the 1960s and 1970s, said Mr Craven.

He said: “Aerospace and space are joined at the hip, and whenever we ask an astronaut to an event, there is a frisson of excitement. People want heroes, and that combination of high-tech and courage is the ultimate inspiration.

“I can remember watching the Moon landing on a black and white TV with a 10-inch screen in the middle of Africa. It’s probably why I ended up as an engineer.”

data.title: Axel Springer wins approval for Telegraph takeover
data.description:
The incoming owner of The Telegraph has revealed ambitions to make it “a truly international brand” after his £575m takeover was waved through.

Mathias Döpfner, the chief executive and controlling shareholder of the German news publisher Axel Springer, assured staff that The Telegraph will retain its “British identity” and that after almost three years of ownership limbo, it “should now take off”.

He welcomed the decision by Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary, to approve the sale without further delays.

Mr Döpfner said: “We are pleased to have received UK government approval to proceed with this acquisition. After a long period of uncertainty, we are able to confirm that we will invest significantly in The Telegraph’s editorial excellence and international growth.”

It came after Ms Nandy told MPs that she had given permission for the Abu Dhabi-backed investor RedBird IMI to sell security in The Telegraph to Axel Springer. The company also owns the specialist titles Politico and Business Insider, as well as scores of print and online publications across Europe.

Mr Döpfner said: “The Telegraph will join a global network committed to independent reporting while retaining its distinct editorial voice and British identity.”

In a memo he told staff he would personally take charge of The Telegraph on Axel Springer’s executive board, calling it “a great privilege – and an even greater responsibility”.

Mr Döpfner added: “What connects us with The Telegraph is not only a shared commitment to freedom and centrist conservative values, but also a distinctive blend of tradition and progress. Both of us have consistently approached technological change and the opportunities of the media industry with a forward-looking mindset.”

The security was set up in 2023 to be immediately converted to full ownership. It means that Axel Springer could be in effective control of The Telegraph within days.

Mr Döpfner, 63, and a journalist by background, sought to reassure staff and readers about Axel Springer’s values and commitment to editorial independence.

He said: “Protecting the newsroom by a clear separation between commercial and editorial decisions – this principle is deeply embedded in our corporate DNA. Decisions about what is published rest with the editors-in-chief. That’s non-negotiable.

“Does that mean that anything goes? No. Our values define the framework. As a journalist and a committed democrat, I hold strong personal views, which I express openly in my writing and in discussion.”

Mr Döpfner added: “However, they do not constitute instructions for our journalists – and everyone who works with us understands this. 

“I value debate in the spirit of pluralism and freedom of expression – especially with our editors-in-chief, all of whom are independent and strong-minded. And sometimes we agree to disagree.”

The decision by Ms Nandy to allow the transfer of security in The Telegraph came alongside a preliminary decision not to trigger a regulatory investigation that would have imposed further months of delay.

She said she was “not minded to” involve the Competition and Markets Authority or Ofcom. The regulators used to scrutinise media takeovers where there are competition or public interest concerns.

Axel Springer is Europe’s largest news publisher, with decades of heritage in independent journalism via German major titles including the tabloid Bild and broadsheet Die Welt. 

Its English-language publications Politico and Business Insider have small market shares in the UK, and lawyers for the company have argued that they do not threaten competition or media plurality.

Ms Nandy did not announce a final decision and reserved the right to change her mind if new information comes to light.

But she said: “I am pleased to be able to take these positive steps, which give greater certainty to The Telegraph and its staff.”

Axel Springer also requires regulatory approval in Ireland and Austria, which may take some weeks. However, the British decision is the critical one and lays the ground for Axel Springer to take effective charge of The Telegraph very soon.

The news was greeted with relief in The Telegraph newsroom. It will all but end a period of damaging uncertainty which began on June 7, 2023, when the Barclay family’s unpaid debts caught up with them.

Lloyds Banking Group seized control of The Telegraph because of £1.2bn in overdue lending to its proprietors, who also owned the parcel courier Yodel and online retailer Very.

The bank planned an auction of The Telegraph, which was derailed by a deal between the Barclay family and the rulers of the United Arab Emirates. The Gulf state paid off its debts in exchange for security over The Telegraph via RedBird IMI, which it majority funded.

Its plan to take full ownership was blocked following a campaign against the takeover by The Telegraph and a cross-party outcry, which led to a ban on foreign state ownership of newspapers.

Without it, The Telegraph would have covered the Iran conflict under the ownership of the UAE. In recent weeks, its authorities have clamped down on free speech, including jailing British visitors for social media posts.

Baroness Stowell of Beeston, a Conservative peer who played a key role in legislating against UAE ownership, said: “Finally, The Telegraph’s ownership has been settled and it is just such great news.

“Here’s to a great future and continued press freedom.”

The decision to block RedBird IMI set off a diplomatic row and a torturous form of corporate captivity, as a series of onward sales were agreed and aborted.

They included one deal with a previously unknown New York publisher and one with the US private equity firm RedBird, which was the UAE’s junior partner. It planned to allow the Gulf state to retain a 15pc stake after Labour relaxed the ban on foreign-state shareholdings. The Telegraph also campaigned against those plans.

When they collapsed in October, Lord Rothermere, the owner of The Daily Mail, agreed to buy The Telegraph in a deal that was set to face lengthy regulatory scrutiny. 

Then, finally, last month, Axel Springer swooped in with a knockout bid of £575m, which appears set to glide through the political and regulatory gauntlet.

The price represents a profit on the £500m paid by RedBird IMI and exceeds most analysts’ estimates of The Telegraph’s value in a rapidly shifting news landscape.

Mr Döpfner arrives on Fleet Street as an established international force. He was appointed chief executive of Axel Springer in 2002 and previously bid for The Telegraph when it was last sold, in 2004.

The company, founded in post-war Berlin, has successfully expanded under his leadership and has said it aims to become the free world’s leading news publisher. Its corporate “constitution” promotes freedom, free speech, free markets, the rule of law and democracy.

Mr Döpfner told The Telegraph staff that he suspected that the company’s eponymous founder “deep down, he would have liked to have been English”.

“In a sense, he now is – four decades after his death,” he added.

data.title: ChatGPT faces criminal investigation over shooting
data.description:
Prosecutors in Florida have launched a criminal investigation into ChatGPT because of claims that the AI bot helped an alleged killer prepare for a mass shooting.

James Uthmeier, Florida’s attorney general, said state officials were demanding answers from OpenAI, the developer of ChatGPT, which allegedly offered “significant advice” to a gunman who left two people dead at Florida State University in April 2025.

“If ChatGPT were a person, it would be facing charges for murder,” Mr Uthmeier said. “This criminal investigation will determine whether OpenAI bears criminal responsibility for ChatGPT’s actions in the shooting at Florida State University last year.”

Mr Uthmeier told a press conference on Wednesday that ChatGPT allegedly “advised the shooter on what type of gun to use, on which ammo went with which gun, on whether or not a gun would be useful in short range”.

He also claimed ChatGPT guided the shooter on when the best time for an attack would be and where on the campus they would encounter the most potential victims.

An OpenAI spokesman said: “ChatGPT is not responsible for this terrible crime.”

Phoenix Ikner, a student at the university, has been charged with killing two people and wounding six after he opened fire with a handgun in the students’ union on April 17 last year. The 21-year-old is facing two murder charges with US officials seeking the death penalty. He has pleaded not guilty.

Under Florida law, anyone who aids, abets or counsels someone who commits a crime can be considered equally responsible.

Florida officials are investigating whether OpenAI bears any criminal responsibility for ChatGPT’s interactions with Mr Ikner before the attack.

Commissioner Mark Glass, of the Florida Department of Law Enforcement, said: “It is important that all are aware of the risks of this new technology, and the harms it can and has already caused in our communities.”

Prosecutors have issued subpoenas to OpenAI seeking all its policies and training material on how to interact with users threatening potential crimes or harm to others.

Growing lawsuits against AI companies

The family of Robert Morales, a 57-year-old man killed in the attack, this month sued OpenAI claiming the shooter had been in “constant communication with ChatGPT”.

AI companies have been accused of failing to spot signs that users are preparing to harm others or themselves and are facing a growing number of lawsuits.

In Canada, the family of a girl critically wounded in a school shooting in Tumbler Ridge, British Columbia, sued OpenAI in March alleging it had “specific knowledge of the shooter utilising ChatGPT to plan a mass casualty event”. OpenAI has said it is making “meaningful changes” to its app to proactively notify police of potential threats.

On the Florida investigation, an OpenAI spokesman said: “Last year’s mass shooting at Florida State University was a tragedy, but ChatGPT is not responsible for this terrible crime.

“After learning of the incident, we identified a ChatGPT account believed to be associated with the suspect and proactively shared this information with law enforcement. We continue to cooperate with authorities.”

The spokesman added: “In this case, ChatGPT provided factual responses to questions with information that could be found broadly across public sources on the internet, and it did not encourage or promote illegal or harmful activity.

“ChatGPT is a general-purpose tool used by hundreds of millions of people every day for legitimate purposes. We work continuously to strengthen our safeguards to detect harmful intent, limit misuse and respond appropriately when safety risks arise.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Transport watchdog director investigated over train ticket expenses
data.description:
Britain’s taxpayer-funded transport watchdog is investigating a member of its board over allegations he wrongly obtained student railcard discounts and double-claimed travel expenses.

Transport Focus, which represents passengers and is funded mainly by a grant from the Department for Transport, is reviewing payments to Rob Wilson, a non-executive director at the watchdog and a former Tory minister. The Cabinet Office is also investigating his expenses.

Records released under the Freedom of Information Act and seen by The Telegraph show that Mr Wilson claimed the cost of two journeys from both Transport Focus and one of his other publicly funded employers.

In November last year, the records show Mr Wilson claimed a £71.30 train ticket and £26 of parking from both Transport Focus and the Social Mobility Commission, a quango sponsored by the Cabinet Office, where he is deputy chairman.

He told each organisation he was travelling for a separate event related to his work for them. Mr Wilson reported to Transport Focus that the expenses were related to a board meeting and to the Social Mobility Commission that they were to attend a “higher education summit”.

The records show he submitted the same picture of the receipt for the Reading to London return ticket for both claims.

Mr Wilson also claimed twice for a £68.20 Reading to London return ticket and £26 parking charge in September 2024. On this occasion, he charged the expenses to Transport Focus and the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), an organisation funded from water bills, where he is chairman.

Again, Mr Wilson gave each organisation different reasons for the expenses. He told Transport Focus that he had travelled for a board meeting and the CCW that he had a meeting with the chairman of Anglian Water.

The Telegraph last month reported on Mr Wilson’s expenses records at the CCW, which showed he had obtained discounts on two first-class train tickets worth a third of their cost using a 16-25 railcard.

The discount brought the cost of first-class tickets under the cost of standard class, making them allowable under the CCW’s expenses policy. The organisation said the former MP, who is 61, had selected the option by mistake in two instances.

His expenses records at Transport Focus and the Social Mobility Commission indicate he made similar errors in his other roles.

He billed Transport Focus for £38.29 last April after booking two journeys with a 16-25 railcard discount. The same month, he also charged the Cabinet Office, which sponsors the Social Mobility Commission, £101.49 for three discounted journeys.

Transport Focus, which last year paid Mr Wilson a fee of £14,000, confirmed this weekend that his expenses claims are under investigation. Claiming a railcard discount fraudulently is a criminal offence and attracts a fine of up to £1,000.

A spokesman said: “We have a process ongoing which reviewed information we received in late March. The process is continuing, reflecting information that has emerged more recently. It would not be appropriate to comment further until that work is complete.”

A Cabinet Office spokesman said: “We were unaware of a duplicate claim by Mr Wilson with Transport Focus until the issue was raised by The Telegraph. We will be investigating this further.”

Lawyers for Mr Wilson said the railcard discounts again “arose from a mistake in his use of the relevant booking app” and its repetition was the result of a default setting.

They added that his double claiming was accidental and that he had intended to split the cost between his employers. Mr Wilson’s lawyers said he was investigating the matter and would ensure that any overpayment was reimbursed.

Mr Wilson, who left Parliament in 2017, also had difficulty selecting the right options while booking flights, according to his Transport Focus records. In 2020 and 2023, he booked and expensed British Airways flights as the Honourable Robert Wilson and The Right Honourable Robert Wilson.

The former honorific is typically used by sitting MPs to refer to each other in parliamentary debates but otherwise mostly applied to the children of viscounts. The Right Honourable is reserved for senior peers and sitting MPs who are members of the Privy Council, on which Mr Wilson never served.

Mr Wilson’s lawyers said the title was mistakenly added to his flight bookings by an administrator. He “had since engaged with British Airways to ensure that this error is corrected”.

It is not the first time that Mr Wilson has encountered controversy over his expenses. In the MPs’ expenses scandal, he was revealed to have claimed nine pence from taxpayers for a 352-yard car journey. He defended the claim, saying the cost of travel “does add up”.

data.title: Tories pledge to drop blanket 20mph speed limits
data.description:
The Conservatives have pledged to scrap so-called “blanket” 20mph speed limits across the country.

Richard Holden, the shadow transport secretary, said on Thursday that Left-wing councils have turned 20mph into the “national speed of bureaucracy”.

Vowing they would be eliminated by Conservative-led councils, he said: “We are ending blanket limits that slow whole towns to a crawl while doing little for real safety.

“Town halls have spent years waging a war on the very people who get up early, drive to work and keep the economy turning.

“Only the Conservatives are prepared to end the war on drivers, rip up the blanket bans, and get Britain moving again for a stronger economy.”

Existing 20mph measures will be reviewed, Mr Holden said, with 30mph limits restored on roads where 20mph limits are widely ignored.

The announcement comes before the council elections on May 7.

Labour-run Wales is the main example of a blanket 20mph speed limit being imposed on Britain’s drivers.

The devolved government in Cardiff introduced and then partially abandoned a blanket 20mph policy on all local roads. It would become the single most unpopular law in the Senedd’s brief history.

It also cost the policy’s architect, Welsh Labour’s Lee Waters, his political career after he resigned in the wake of the backlash to its introduction.

Although Ken Skates, his successor, announced that the blanket ban would be lifted, few councils have raised their speed limits to 30mph. Only in areas outside schools and hospitals must the speed limit remain at 20mph in Wales.

The experiment left half a million people facing fines for breaking the lowered limit, as The Telegraph reported last year.

Current government guidance to councils, last updated in March 2024 – before Labour’s general election victory in July that year – says: “Traffic authorities should use the right speed limits in the right places. They should keep their speed limits under review and only introduce 20mph limits and zones in the right places, over time and with local support in urban areas and built-up village streets that are primarily residential.”

The Conservatives’ Plan for Drivers, published in 2023, set out the principle that 20mph limits should be targeted and evidence-led rather than imposed as “blanket measures”, a party spokesman said.

Mr Holden added: “The previous Conservative government was clear in our guidance that blanket approaches were wrong. Labour ignored it and their councils kept rolling them out in the teeth of public opposition.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Losses hit £53m at The Sun as it battles falling tabloid sales
data.description:
The Sun has reported a £53m loss as Rupert Murdoch’s newspaper battles falling tabloid sales.

Newly filed accounts for News Group Newspapers (NGN), which publishes The Sun and Sun on Sunday, show pre-tax losses widened from £18m in the year to July 2025.

It came as turnover fell by 7pc to £273m, which bosses blamed on a drop in print sales and algorithm changes that dented online readership.

The figures underscore the challenge facing The Sun, which has been grappling with how to restore profitability across its advertising-funded business model.

The Sun introduced a premium model last year, offering readers access to exclusive stories and columns by star writers such as Jeremy Clarkson for £2 a month.

The move, which mirrored a similar partial paywall launched by MailOnline, was aimed at reducing the title’s reliance on dwindling ad revenues.

However, the rollout of AI tools in Google’s search engine is posing a fresh threat to free-to-access online journalism, with a number of publishers already reporting sharp reductions in traffic.

The tabloid has also sought to offset declining readership by expanding in the US, while it has also made redundancies to cut costs.

The publisher employed 530 jobs at the end of the last financial year, down from 552 a year previously.

The Sun reported increased legal costs related to the phone-hacking scandal at The News of the World, which more than doubled last year to £36.7m.

This is thought to include a £10m settlement with the Duke of Sussex, which was struck with NGN last year. The publisher issued an apology for “serious intrusion” by The Sun and phone hacking by private investigators working for the News of the World.

The troubles at The Sun stand in contrast to Mr Murdoch’s broadsheet titles, which operate under a subscription model.

Revenues at The Times and Sunday Times increased to £390m, driven by growth in subscribers and advertising. The newspaper has been seeking to boost reader numbers by reducing the total number of stories it publishes, in a strategy it claims should result in “better stories”.

Decline of TalkTV

Separate accounts showed Mr Murdoch had lost more than £150m on his failed news channel TalkTV in a rare setback for the media mogul.

TalkTV, the opinionated broadcaster that was pulled off air two years ago, posted an operating loss of £8.7m in the year to July 2025.

It takes cumulative losses since the channel’s launch in 2022 to more than £150m. Mr Murdoch’s News Corp also booked a £25m writedown on its investments in the UK, which it said was in response to challenging market conditions affecting its broadcasting operations.

A £154m loan to TalkTV has been waived and the channel’s parent company has now ceased trading.

The demise of TalkTV marks a rare defeat for the nonagenarian tycoon, who last year handed control of his news empire to his eldest son, Lachlan Murdoch, after a bitter succession battle.

The deal will secure the Right-wing stance of Mr Murdoch’s outlets, which include Fox and the Wall Street Journal as well as his UK newspapers. Lachlan is closely aligned with his father’s politics, whereas siblings James, Elisabeth and Prudence are viewed as more liberal.

Mr Murdoch was separately handed a boost this week as a US judge dismissed Donald Trump’s $10bn (£7.4bn) lawsuit against The Wall Street Journal for publishing a lewd birthday note sent by the president to Jeffrey Epstein.

A spokesman for News UK said: “News UK is a portfolio of assets, each at a different level of digital maturity. 

“Those brands are managed as a whole and our investments are focused on formats which will deliver revenues over the long term to create a sustainable future for all of our journalism.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Shoplifting surges by 133pc in five years
data.description:
The number of shoplifting offences has risen by 133 per cent in the past five years, official figures show.

The number of thefts reported to police rose from 228,128 in 2020-21 to 530,457 in 2024-25, according to House of Commons Library data analysed by the Liberal Democrats.

However, only 19.83 per cent of offences in 2024-25 resulted in a charge, with wide variations between the 43 police forces in England and Wales.

At the bottom of the table was the Metropolitan Police, with fewer than 7 per cent of recorded cases resulting in a charge.

This was down from 8.64 per cent in 2020-21, when there were 31,008 shoplifting offences reported to the force. Since then, the number of store thefts has tripled to 93,626.

Durham Constabulary brought charges in 32.7 per cent of its shoplifting cases, more than four times the rate of the Met in 2024-25.

It was followed at the top of the table by Humberside (31.9 per cent), Norfolk (30.6 per cent), Cumbria (30.44 per cent) and West Mercia (30.21 per cent).

Marks & Spencer has criticised the failure of the Government and police to get a grip on shoplifting, which the retailer claimed had seen attacks on its stores by thieves become “more brazen, more organised and more aggressive”.

Executives from the store wrote to Sir Sadiq Khan, the Mayor of London, and Shabana Mahmood, the Home Secretary, demanding they do more to address the problem.

The Lib Dems said small businesses “simply cannot afford for this to continue” and called for a return to community policing with dedicated police counters.

‘Our communities deserve better’

Max Wilkinson, the Lib Dems’ home affairs spokesman, said: “Our communities deserve better than the surge of shoplifting that’s ransacking our local high streets. For years, the Conservatives quietly dismantled community policing and this Labour Government has failed to properly restore it.

“Retail workers have, in too many cases, been left to fend for themselves as hundreds of thousands of shoplifters get away scot-free. Our brilliant small businesses simply cannot afford for this to continue.”

He added: “Liberal Democrats have time and again said we need a visible neighbourhood police presence, with staffed police counters in every community and officers on the ground. Only then can we make our high streets safe for shopkeepers and shoppers.”

Labour has committed to ensuring every community has a named contactable officer, and pledged to put 13,000 extra police officers back on the streets by the end of this Parliament.

Its new crime and policing bill also aims to curb rising shoplifting by repealing the “low-value” theft rule, which treats shoplifted items below £200 as minor summary offences. It also introduces a separate offence for assaulting retail workers and new respect orders to ban repeat offenders from town centres.

Earlier this week, the Met’s head of business crime insisted his force was determined to come down hard on shoplifting, having already nearly doubled detection rates, increased arrests of store thieves by 44 per cent and seen a 3.2 per cent drop in offences in London in the past year.

The Lib Dems have also raised concerns that shoplifting is particularly hitting small businesses, which are already under pressure from rising energy bills, business rates and extra costs from the Chancellor’s National Insurance contribution rise.

The party has called for a 5p VAT cut for hospitality businesses and urgent reform of the business rates system to breathe life into high streets.

A Home Office spokesman said: “This Government inherited unacceptable levels of shop theft. Real progress is being made, with the number of people caught and charged rising by 21 per cent.

“Alongside major policing reforms to boost neighbourhood patrols, supported by 13,000 extra neighbourhood officers, we are giving police the powers they need to tackle shop theft, including scrapping the £200 rule that lets too many offenders off the hook.”

data.title: Billionaire Weston family to spin off Primark
data.description:
The billionaire family behind Primark is to spin off the fashion chain from its food business, breaking up one of Britain’s biggest consumer conglomerates after 65 years.

Associated British Foods (ABF) said it planned to split Primark off by the end of 2027, paving the way for a separate listing on the FTSE 100.

ABF is listed on the London Stock Exchange but around 60pc of its shares are owned by the Weston family’s Wittington Investments. The conglomerate is one of the world’s biggest sugar producers and also owns brands including Ovaltine, Hovis and Twinings.

Analysts have predicted that a separate listing of Primark, which has 486 stores across 19 markets, could see it valued at a similar level to rivals such as Next, which is worth more than £16bn.

George Weston, the chief executive and a member of the controlling Weston family, will become chief executive of the food business, while Eoin Tonge will continue to lead Primark who took over as interim chief executive last year.

Mr Weston said there were “a lot of good things that will come from this separation”, adding that Primark was “very capable of standing on its own two feet” and would benefit from having a board of non-executives who had specific retail experience.

He said ABF’s food business, meanwhile, had been “under appreciated because most of the interest has inevitably been around Primark”.

Plans to press ahead with the separation come despite warnings from ABF that the war in Iran was likely to hit consumer spending.

Mr Weston said there was a “risk to Primark sales if the conflict persists and consumer spending deteriorates”.

“Based on the last couple of weeks, we’ve seen sales right across Europe reduce, but we just don’t know,” he said. “Some of our customers may stop shopping, but some other customers might start shopping at Primark because they see value there.”

Mr Weston said inflation from the Middle East conflict was likely to be “manageable” this year. ABF had locked in energy prices in advance, helping to limit the impact of the shock.

By July and August, though, he said ABF would “become increasingly exposed to the higher costs, should they still be in place”.

The situation “might resolve itself before those extra costs come through the system”, he added.

Mr Weston said ABF had not yet seen any disruption to shipments. One vessel carrying its goods passed through the Suez Canal last weekend. Some freight companies have been avoiding the Egyptian waterway over fears of spillover from the conflict nearby.

Shares in ABF slumped as much as 5pc to £17.90 on Tuesday. Analysts at Barclays said this was likely driven by the “cautious commentary” about Primark sales, as well as weaker-than-expected profits in its sugar business.

ABF posted a 2pc fall in revenue to just under £9.5bn in the 24 weeks ending February, while profit declined by 9pc to £632m.

data.title: UK airlines call for emergency jet fuel plan to avert summer chaos
data.description:
Britain’s airlines have called on the Government to present an emergency jet fuel plan or risk a summer of holiday chaos.

Airlines UK said ministers must prepare for fuel shortages now if disruption to flights is to be avoided. The association’s members include British Airways, Virgin Atlantic and easyJet.

It came as Lufthansa, Germany’s biggest airline, cancelled 20,000 flights in one of the biggest cuts of the crisis so far. The cuts to short-haul routes within Europe will last from May until October and save the airline 40,000 metric tons of jet fuel.

ACI Europe, which represents airports, has warned that some hubs may begin to run short of jet fuel by the middle of May, following the closure of vital supply lines during the Iran war.

Airlines UK urged Britain to bolster fuel reserves by forcing refineries to produce more kerosene and temporarily allowing US-grade fuel, which can be used by some planes, to be imported.

It called for Labour to cushion cost increases with measures including tax cuts and the suspension of stringent environmental regulations imposed on carriers.

The group warned about the “immediate impact on the UK aviation sector and UK consumers in the event disruption to jet fuel supply continues or worsens”.

Airlines UK’s demands, made in a paper sent to ministers and the Civil Aviation Authority, will be seen as an indication of the level of concern among airlines as they face dwindling fuel reserves. The Strait of Hormuz, which has been blockaded during the Middle East conflict, was a vital shipping route for 20pc of the world’s oil and gas volumes.

The paper, seen by The Telegraph, warned that costs for airlines were already rising faster in the UK than on the Continent before the Gulf crisis. The industry group pointed to increases in the air passenger duty departure tax and the imposition of a tougher mandate for the use of expensive sustainable aviation fuel.

It added: “Doubling of jet fuel costs – with fuel around one third of airline costs – represents a major, additional price shock.”

UK must ‘take right actions now’

Among other demands, Airlines UK called for an update of the “national emergency plan for fuel” to set out allocation plans for aviation fuel, not just petrol and diesel.

It said airlines should also be exempted from passenger compensation rules governing flight cancellations – not only when fuel runs short but also if they modify timetables to reflect changes in demand.

A spokesman for Airlines UK said: “It is vital that the Government takes the right actions now to ensure the continuation of supply, as well as support the UK aviation industry which has been impacted by record high jet fuel prices.

“Additional flexibility, should it be needed, would enable airlines to operate most efficiently to minimise any disruption to customers and maintain long-established global networks.”

BA’s owner, International Airlines Group, said its carriers were “not immune” to the jump in fuel costs even with a hedging strategy which provided near-term protection from price rises.

A spokesman said: “If these pressures continue, flexibility from government would ensure airlines can continue to operate as efficiently as possible and manage sustained cost challenges while keeping people and trade moving.”

Apostolos ⁠Tzitzikostas, the EU transport commissioner, said on Tuesday that guidance was being prepared for EU airlines on how to handle airport slots, passenger rights and public ​service obligations in the ‌event of fuel shortages.

The ‌commission is ‌due to present ⁠a broader package of energy and transport measures on Wednesday.

Airlines UK said: “If the EU provides guidance on some of these critical areas, we need the UK to set out where they too sit as soon as possible.”
