data.title: Leonard Licht, brilliant stock-picker for pension funds at Warburgs and Mercury Asset Management
data.description:
Leonard Licht, who has died aged 81, was one of London’s most influential but idiosyncratic investors as a senior executive of Mercury Asset Management.

Mercury began life as the investment division of the merchant bank SG Warburg & Co, which Leonard Licht joined as a teenage clerk in 1963. He went on to make a name as a brilliant stock-picker and to be co-leader of the firm’s pension fund team – the most powerful in the City – when Mercury was floated as a separate company in 1987.

Licht’s major clients, for whom he produced outstanding returns over the years, were the pension funds of the BBC and British Rail; the latter, according to a colleague, “thought he walked on water”. Combining encyclopaedic knowledge of British companies with an intuitive sense of value and an intolerance for stockbrokers’ waffle, he bought shares he liked in unusually large portions.

These included significant stakes in the shipping and property group P&O, Burton Menswear, House of Fraser department stores and the chinaware manufacturer Wedgwood, of which Mercury at one time held almost a quarter. When a clutch of privatised regional water companies floated simultaneously on the stock exchange in December 1989, Licht bought 10 per cent of the whole list.

Exercising emotional intelligence rather than dry analysis, Licht won new clients not with slideshows on investment strategy but by telling them simply that he preferred shares “which go up”. His picks did so far more often than they let him down.                

Leonard Samuel Licht was born in London on March 15 1945, the only child of Bernhard Licht and his wife Hilde, née Muller. Bernhard’s family had been furniture manufacturers at Glogau in Lower Silesia (then in Germany, now in Poland), but the couple fled the Nazi regime in 1938 to arrive in England with 10 shillings each. Bernhard was interned on the Isle of Man in 1940 but went on to build a business with his brother Herbert as export agents for textile manufacturers.

Leonard was educated at Christ’s College in Finchley, where by his own account he was a “very idle” pupil. He nevertheless became an avid reader and, “just after I learned to walk”, a collector first of bus tickets of different colours according to the fare and later, encouraged by birthday presents from a grandfather, of stamps.

Leaving school in 1963 with one A-level, Licht wrote on spec to ask for a job at Warburgs – where he had a family introduction – and was taken on as a clerk at £360 a year. His first task for the investment team was to file company reports and Extel cards, the principal source of financial updates in the pre-electronic age, and to man the phones during the short lunchtime permitted under austere Warburg rules.

Though he enjoyed spotting curiosities in the daily data flow, Licht was an awkward employee who twice survived being asked to leave the firm before he found a niche as an assistant fund manager and went on to join the team responsible for Warburgs’ growing pension business.

He operated in a style all his own, commuting to work in a chauffeured Rolls-Royce. Prone to temper and mood swings, his impatience with a Hong Kong tailor’s tape measure caused a consignment of 10 bespoke suits to be dismissed as “a disaster” and passed to his chauffeur, whom they did not fit at all.

If Licht loved and to some extent personified the culture of Warburgs and Mercury, he was neither a company man nor a conventional team player. He particularly disliked committee meetings and the increasingly burdensome bureaucracy of compliance. Though he eventually held the title of vice-chairman, he was best left flying solo.

In the summer of 1992 he took his co-directors by surprise when he abruptly resigned to join Jupiter Tyndall, an up-and-coming fund boutique – led by John Duffield, whose maverick investing style matched his own – in which Licht had bought a small stake on Mercury’s behalf a few months earlier.

Mercury declined to present Licht’s departure as amicable, though personal friendships were restored after a period of coolness. Nor were his three years with Jupiter notably happy; he resigned in 1996 to pursue his own interests, later returning to the fray as chairman of HG, a private equity business that had originated in Mercury before being spun out by Merrill Lynch, which bought Mercury in 1997.

Younger HG colleagues warmly recalled his influenc​e and encouragement, and the enjoyment of outings with him to Borough Market.

Among Licht’s private investments was a stake in Falkland Island Holdings, whose assets included the port of Stanley and its only hotel. After retiring, he told friends he had sold all his shares and returned to his passion for stamps and coins.

He and his wife Judith, née Grossman, whom he married in 1973, were a devoted couple. They lived for some years in Monaco before returning to London, and wintered in Miami Beach. She survives him with their son and daughter.        

Leonard Licht, born March 15 1945, died March 25 2026​

data.title: Britain’s £8bn bet on the developing world
data.description:
Britain will invest up to £8bn in companies across the developing world over the next five years, in a bid to make a return for the UK taxpayer while driving global development.

The initiative is being led by British International Investment (BII), a government owned UK investment vehicle founded in 1948 to boost agricultural production across the empire.

At the time, Britain faced food shortages and rationing in the wake of the Second World War.

Jenny Chapman, the UK Minister for Development, now wants to accelerate the flow of capital to developing countries via BII as UK overseas direct aid budgets are shrinking.

It is envisaged that for every pound of public money BII invests a matched contribution will come from the private sector, bringing the total investment to about £15 billion.

Leslie Maasdorp, the South Africa born Chief Executive of BII, said: “Britain has a proud history of acting as a global leader in international development. BII is committed to demonstrating that a new, mutually-beneficial path exists that creates secure jobs and stable economic conditions in the countries in which we invest, while providing value for money for the UK taxpayer”.

Investing in developing economies is high risk but can also bring in substantial financial rewards beyond the help it provides locally in terms of innovation and jobs.

Over the last decade, BII generated a £1.6 billion return on investments, equating to a return of around five per cent a year. The new five year cycle envisages a minimum return on capital of at least two per cent but managers will be hoping for more.

The fund’s capital will also support UK based companies that are delivering positive economic and environmental outcomes in developing markets.

In an interview with the Telegraph, Mr Maasdorp said that development and national security were “reinforcing” concepts and “not opposites”. 

“The sources of uncontrolled migration flows, the sources of illicit finance, the sources of terrorism, extremism… they come from these challenging environments,” he said.

“If the global north is not engaged in investing in and stabilising these places, it impacts on the well being of the UK because the world is so interconnected.

“We cannot solve the global challenges we all face – poverty, instability, conflict and global public health – without bringing the least developed countries with us on the journey to shared prosperity.”

China has become the world’s biggest state investor in developing economies over the last 20 years, making huge investments in infrastructure across Asia and Africa.

Its strategy has been guided by the Belt and Road initiative, which aims to forge new trade corridors and dramatically boost China’s influence in global affairs.

All over Africa, Chinese construction companies with excess capacity and expertise developed during China’s own boom have been building ports, dams, roads, railways, schools, hospitals and solar power systems across the continent.

Mr Maasdorp said the approach to investment being pursued by BII and other western state-backed investors was different to the Chinese approach. 

It was not “state-led” and, while UK companies could be utilised where appropriate, BII’s strategy was not dependent on them. 

“Our model is embedded in the belief that the private sector is the engine of economic growth,” he said. “The state has an important and vital role to play in creating enabling conditions for investment… but ultimately it is the private sector and entrepreneurs that make an economy tick”. 

He added that long term “strategic relationships” with similar state-backed investment funds across the west gave BII and its partners the financial heft to compete effectively with China, he added.

BII has the freedom to invest in any enterprise that drives development and provides a public good, but it focuses on those countries most in need of investment.

Between 2026-2031, at least 40 per cent of BII’s total new commitments by value will be in climate finance – a sector that China has become dangerously dominant in globally.

BII is also a founding member of the 2X Challenge which has raised over $33.6 billion to empower women’s economic development.

But its assets are varied.

Current investments include the financing of a new deepwater container port in the Congo, an extension of the partnership that commenced with the modernisation and expansion of ports in Senegal, Egypt and Somaliland.

The new port development is expected to enable the creation of approximately 85,000 jobs and around $1.12 billion in additional trade and $429m in increased economic output annually.

BII was also a founder investor in Safaricom Ethiopia which was awarded Ethiopia’s first private mobile network licence.

Since its launch in 2022, the cost of mobile data services in the country has fallen by up to 70 per cent and, by June 2024, the company had signed up 4.4 million customers. 

Like an increasing number of international investors, Mr Maasdorp is bullish on the long term prospects for the continent of Africa – the world’s last great underdeveloped region.

It has the world’s fastest growing population, huge mineral wealth and enough farmland to become the “breadbasket of the world,” he says.

Its governance has improved dramatically over the last 25 years and, if African economies can “add value” by processing what they produce rather than simply exporting, it will boom just as the so-called Asian Tigers did in the 1990s.

“There are lots of pockets of positive momentum which could change the prospects of Africa all around,” said Mr Maasdorp.

 “With an average age of 18 or 19, the energy of youth is marked. And what the world of social media has done is give people a sense of what is possible – aspiration.

“Tech and AI companies are developing now in Africa, which I think holds great potential for many sectors to leapfrog ahead as they are building, in many cases, from scratch.”

data.title: Elon Musk’s SpaceX plots $60bn deal for AI start-up
data.description:
Elon Musk’s SpaceX is lining up a $60bn (£44bn) deal to buy a four-year-old artificial intelligence coding start-up as the rocket business prepares for a blockbuster stock market listing.

The space business has announced it has secured an option to buy Silicon Valley start-up Cursor later this year, or agree to spend $10bn on its coding tools as part of a partnership.

Cursor is a so-called “vibe coding” business that allows programmers to generate entire websites and apps using chatbots.

Customers can use Cursor to access bots such as OpenAI’s ChatGPT or Anthropic’s Claude.

The San Francisco business, led by Michael Truell, 25, was founded in 2022 and has fewer than 400 staff, according to the start-up database Pitchbook.

However, it is on the brink of being sold to SpaceX as Mr Musk seeks to regain momentum in the AI race after falling behind the likes of OpenAI and Anthropic.

The world’s richest man merged xAI, his AI business that controls the Grok chatbot, with SpaceX earlier this year. That deal valued xAI at $250bn and represented the biggest private merger ever.

However, this latest proposed acquisition will represent one of the largest ever struck by Mr Musk outside of his own empire. He bought Twitter in a highly leveraged $44bn takeover in 2022.

A deal with Cursor would allow SpaceX to tap the rapidly growing market for AI developer tools that have surged in popularity in Silicon Valley.

This is expected to bolster SpaceX’s growth as it races to launch data centre satellites into space to provide computing firepower for its businesses.

“SpaceXAI and Cursor AI are now working closely together to create the world’s best coding and knowledge work AI,” SpaceX said on Tuesday.

It said the deal could bring SpaceX a “leading product and distribution to expert software engineers”.

Cursor was most recently valued at $29bn after raising more than $2.3bn from investors in November. It has recently been one of the fastest-growing companies in Silicon Valley.

SpaceX has been ramping up its preparations for a record-breaking $1.75bn listing later this year.

It is planning to raise as much as $75bn in the deal, in what would be the biggest stock market debut of any company.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Britain in danger of outsourcing its intellect, warns AI pioneer
data.description:
The artificial intelligence revolution threatens to erode Britain’s sovereignty, one of the country’s most experienced tech entrepreneurs has warned.

Ken Mulvany, co-founder and executive chairman of Benevolent AI, which has pioneered artificial intelligence in drug discovery, told The Telegraph that the United States and China are increasingly dominant in the crucial field.

He says: “If you look at this from the UK or Europe’s perspective, the risk is that these – China and America – are now the two cognitive powers. They’re the ones that are investing the most into this.

“I do worry about having the nation’s intellect effectively outsourced. It’s not just that, either. It’s the productivity gains.

“If for every hour of cognitive work that you do there’s a machine doing what would take you 50 hours, how do you as a nation compete with that?

“Electricity multiplied the power of muscles and AI can do the same with brains in certain scenarios. But it’s almost like you, as Britain and Europe are outsourcing national IQ to US and Chinese tech companies.

“So you’re left trying to regulate something you don’t own. And I do worry about that. I think of it as cognitive border control.”

Mulvany is better placed than most to sound the alarm. An eerily youthful 57 who betrays his Hawaiian roots with a surf-ready hairdo, he is an entrepreneur who has seen tech boom and bust more than once.

In a world increasingly haunted by visions of an AI apocalypse that would make the dotcom crash look like a minor market setback, Mulvany and Benevolent have already been tested to near-destruction.

The technology excited investors – including the star fund manager Neil Woodford long before his self-inflicted implosion – enough to snag a £1.5bn valuation way back in 2018.

This was long before the likes of Sam Altman of OpenAI and Dario Amodei of Anthropic emerged to seek thrones as the next generation of tech plutocrats.

Benevolent sought to help researchers to quickly design new drugs with computer assistance, using machine learning techniques to scour a treasure trove of science information for possibilities at an unprecedented clip. Expansion into defence and energy markets promised new horizons and riches.

It seemed to work. Benevolent burst onto the public markets long before the current AI frontrunners had attracted the attention of everyday investors, too. With the world still in the grip of the pandemic and having discovered an anti-inflammatory that was proving useful on pandemic wards, the start-up made a splashy debut.

“I wasn’t really part of it at that time,” Mulvany recalls ruefully. Having seemingly set up Benevolent for enormous success, he was already easing into semi-retirement.

As it secured a lucrative listing in Amsterdam, Benevolent won endorsement and investment from some of the pharmaceutical industry’s biggest names. AstraZeneca backed Benevolent as part of a transaction that raised hundreds of millions.

Alas, Mulvany’s big payday and exit did not set the scene for a sustainable company. A starry board and big executive hires steered Benevolent off a cliff.

The company lost almost all its value within two years, prompting Mulvany to abandon his early retirement and mount a boardroom coup to take back control last year. He had become frustrated that Benevolent had shifted from his concept of a technology company to a drug developer in its own right, with all the risk and costs associated with it.

“They brought in some luminaries,” Mulvany recalls. “Some really heavy hitters from pharma. And Benevolent morphed from a tech company to a pharma company. For me as an investor, and for some of my fellow investors, we didn’t have the appetite for that. We were technology investors.

“You had a technology shareholder base and a management and board doing biotech. All of its revenue was from its technology but it was spending a ton of money developing drugs.

“The technology cost was very low relative to drug development. So I became more of an activist shareholder at that point because it wasn’t going the way I wanted it to go and the markets reflected that too. It lost 95pc of its value.”

It’s a tale of hype meeting with financial and scientific reality that now seems to be playing out on an extreme scale as OpenAI and its rivals test the limits of pre-profit funding and investor patience. Altman alone has committed to spending more than $1tn (£750bn).

Having abandoned his retirement to mount his successful coup, Mulvany is now rebuilding Benevolent as an enterprise software company. Its new software and services are focused sharply on helping drug developers, rather than developing drugs. It’s a more commercial proposition, with a closer eye on returns for Benevolent and its big pharma customers.

“We help on the science side of things, reviewing the literature, but also how you make business decisions as a drug developer, how you expand into a particular market, how you design trials and all of that.

“People in the industry are making decisions with a fraction of the information they need.”

These are still the early days of Benevolent’s attempted comeback and Mulvany’s unexpected return from early retirement, which he had intended to spend skiing. His hopes of using the company’s targeted AI technology beyond drug discovery in fields such as defence and energy have been revived.

Benevolent, without revenue, now has the cash and investor support to pursue its new business into 2027, according to Mulvany.

Despite the mounting fears of an AI bust, he remains a believer in the technology. After a deep cull of staff, Benevolent’s coders are 50 times faster with the help of computer intelligence, for instance. But Mulvany, now an AI survivor, admits a shake-out lies ahead for those with grandiose visions.

“I saw this in the dotcom. I was there right at the bubble. But whether it’s railroads, or electricity or the internet there has always been over-investment and hype.

“So there’s going to be losers in this but the infrastructure is being put in place. So yeah, you’re seeing an AI bubble, but I’ve seen it before.”

data.title: Middle classes risk paying more for their shopping under surge pricing
data.description:
Middle-class households may have to pay more for their shopping as retailers roll out surge pricing, Bank of England economists have warned.

New AI-pricing systems may charge better-off households more in order to subsidise lower prices for poorer families, researchers wrote on the Bank’s blog.

The rise of algorithmic and electronic pricing means retailers are increasingly able to personalise prices for different customers, based on characteristics such as what they can afford or particularly need.

Anthony Savagar, Misa Tanaka and Jagdish Tripathy, all Bank of England economists, wrote: “This may lead to higher price dispersion as individuals with high willingness-to-pay subsidise those with lower willingness-to-pay.”

In practice, this is likely to mean better-off households paying more for items than those who are worse off.

Retailers could charge different prices depending on where a person lives. Research in the US shows that shops there already do this.

Mr Savagar wrote: “With increased availability of big data and computing power, more firms are adopting algorithmic and AI-powered pricing to adjust prices rapidly in response to changing economic conditions over time and across consumers.”

Clare Lombardelli, a senior Bank of England official, warned earlier this month of “concerns about fairness” as surge pricing could soon be coming to supermarkets.

A report in February by think tank Onward found that Britons were already spending £10bn a year on subsidies for people claiming Universal Credit through discounts on services for benefit claimants.

These include reduced broadband tariffs, council tax discounts and prescription charge exemptions, which are subsidised by the people paying the full costs.

Households living on benefits can also get major discounts on experiences such as visits to London Zoo, Westminster Abbey and the Tower of London, where a family of four can get a £107 discount worth 96pc of their ticket price.

The rise of big data and technology such as electronic shelf labels means that retailers are increasingly using AI algorithms to adjust prices rapidly based on supply and demand.

Businesses are also exploring personalised pricing, where they tailor prices to each individual customer, based on their circumstances or interests. In practice, this could mean charging a person more for a band T-shirt if streaming data showed they were one of the group’s biggest fans.

Mr Savagar warned that the rise of electronic and algorithmic systems could also make inflation shocks more intense as prices rise almost immediately.

Price surges could also become steeper because the algorithms could push shops to “raise markups more aggressively when inflation is already high”, he said.

data.title: Ryanair to shut check-in 20 minutes early amid EU border delays
data.description:
Ryanair is to close its check-in desks 20 minutes early as the EU’s new border controls lead to lengthy delays for passengers.

The discount airline currently allows passengers to check-in and drop their bags until 40 minutes before their flights. 

However, from November desks will close an hour before take-off, meaning travellers will have to arrive earlier to avoid missing the window. 

The move aims to ensure that Ryanair customers who check in a bag make it to the gate on time, rather than being delayed by security and passport checks.

It comes as the introduction of the EU’s Entry/Exit System (EES) – which collects fingerprints and facial scans from visitors from outside the bloc – has caused significant disruption at some European airports, with long queues that have led some Britons to miss their flights home.

More than 100 passengers missed an easyJet flight this month owing to passport queues in Milan. The airline has a 40-minute check-in deadline in the Italian city, though the majority of its desks operate a 60-minute cut-off – such as the rules being imposed by Ryanair.

In response to the meltdown, Greece defied the EU this week by dropping biometric checks for UK visitors, who make a major contribution to the country’s tourism-dependent economy.

The EES scheme is one of a number of EU rules adding to holiday red tape. Pet owners have been warned they can no longer use pet passports when travelling to the Continent, unless they live in Europe full-time.

Ryanair, which carries 200 million passengers a year, referred to the tighter check-in deadline as an “improvement”, even though it will require passengers to spend more time at the airport.

The airline has a reputation for strict cabin-luggage rules, with Michael O’Leary, its chief executive, raising bonuses last year for staff who fine passengers for oversized bags, allowing them to earn an extra €1,000 (£850) a year by identifying one rule-breaker per day.

Ryanair also banned paper boarding passes last November, requiring people to generate digital passes using its app after checking in online. Age UK said the move ignored the four million Britons who have never used the internet, and the millions more who do not own a smartphone.

Dara Brady, Ryanair’s chief marketing officer, said the check-in change would ensure that people needing to drop off bags would make it to their flight even during busy travel periods when queues are longer.

He said: “This will allow the 20pc of our customers who check in a bag more time to clear through airport security and passport queues, and get to their departure gate on time.”

Ryanair said it aimed to install self-service kiosks at more than 95pc of the airports it serves by October, in preparation for the new check-in rules which will be enforced from November.

Passengers with cabin bags will continue to check in online before they arrive at the airport, and then go straight through security to their gate.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Why Britain faces the growing risk of Spanish-style blackouts
data.description:
The great British blackout was once a threat that hung over the country during the depths of winter, when generators struggled to meet demand or storms destroyed power lines.

But today, as the net zero revolution rolls on, the greatest threat to grid stability could land on a sunny Sunday afternoon.

According to the National Energy System Operator (Neso), the body responsible for keeping the lights on, the UK power grid is at increasing risk of being overwhelmed by surges in solar power.

Underpinning this threat is the fact that too many solar panels have been connected to the grid without any way of monitoring them or controlling their output.

As a result, a typically sunny day in summer can lead to nearly 19 gigawatts (GW) of power pouring into the UK’s networks, while only 12-15GW is being consumed.

That imbalance is a recipe for disaster because significant destabilisation of the grid can cause blackouts.

Such a calamity struck Spain and Portugal last year, when a chain reaction triggered by solar farms knocked out power to 55 million people.

At the time, solar and wind generated 80pc of energy in the Iberian Peninsula, the result of renewables displacing power stations run by nuclear and gas.

While a welcome switch for supporters of green energy, the removal of traditional power stations can prove problematic – as Spain and Portugal discovered last year.

This is because of their importance in providing vital system stability, known as inertia.

Inertia is created by generators with spinning parts – such as turbines running on gas, coal or hydropower – which wind and solar do not have.

This form of kinetic energy helps to prevent sudden changes in a system’s frequency.

Therefore, when two solar farms suddenly disconnected from Spain’s grid last year, there was no steady inertia to keep the country’s power networks afloat.

In practice, Neso is confident the UK will never see such a disaster. However, it admits the challenge is growing as ever more solar farms are built in Ed Miliband’s net zero drive.

“We are confident that we have the tools required to operate the system safely and reliably,” Neso said in its Summer Outlook report.

“But there may be periods when balancing supply, demand and system needs will require us to use our full range of operational tools, including system notices.”

However, independent experts are not so sure – and we may soon find out who is right. The crunch will probably come around the late May bank holiday, or on any sunny summer Sunday.

Last year, that date saw national demand for power plummet to a record low of 11GW, while sun and wind drove up power generation to nearly 19GW. And crucially, Neso could do little to stop it.

This sense of powerlessness is new. In the past, the grid operator’s response to an excess of power was simply to switch off some power stations.

But Neso’s problem is that it can no longer see what is happening on the UK’s power networks.

Unlike before, energy is being created naturally from wind and solar farms that Neso cannot control.

Underlying Britain’s energy grid are two main power networks.

At the top is the high-voltage transmission grid, which takes electricity from power stations to cities, towns and industry.

Everything on it is closely measured and monitored from Neso’s Electricity National Control Centre in Wokingham, Berkshire.

There, controllers use a giant wall-to-wall digital map of the transmission network to match supply to demand – switching power stations on or reducing output as needed.

Below that lies the distribution network, which takes electricity from the transmission grid, reduces its voltage, and pumps it into our homes and businesses.

It was designed to be a passive power carrier, meaning it lacks most of the switching or measuring systems found on the transmission grid. Ultimately, this makes it hard to monitor or control.

However, in the rush for decarbonisation, officials have been hooking the distribution system up to hundreds of new power sources, particularly solar farms.

Almost all of those solar generators are largely invisible to Neso’s operators, and they cannot see how much power is being generated or switch it off.

This now poses a problem, according to Simon Gallagher, the chief executive of UK Network Services, a leading consultancy.

“Traditionally, most power generators were connected to the transmission system and ‘dispatched’ centrally, so Neso can instruct them to turn on, turn off, ramp up and down to balance the grid in real time,” he says.

“Big power stations with large gas generators get tuned up and down as Neso monitors changes in demand.

“But solar is almost all connected to the distribution system and Neso has no control or even visibility of what it is doing. It means there is a risk of imbalances. On a sunny summer Sunday, demand will be very low, but solar will be generating at full capacity.”

However, whether this results in a blackout similar to Spain is another matter.

Some industry experts say an energy crisis is unlikely in the UK because its grid is structured differently, but regional failures and high costs remain a threat.

“Britain’s solar surplus problem is more about balancing costs than sudden system failure,” says Timothy Santonastaso, the deputy head of European power pricing at the consultancy Argus.

Such problems should have been addressed sooner, according to Claire Coutinho, the energy secretary in the last Conservative government and now a shadow minister.

‘Bogus numbers’

While in power, Coutinho says she became so worried about the impact of excessive solar disrupting the grid that she ordered civil servants to investigate.

This involved a review of the costs associated with balancing the grid when it is too windy or sunny.

Last year, the Government spent more than £1bn on paying wind farms to switch off in so-called curtailment costs.

“I started a full systems cost of wind and solar in the department because we need to know the true costs of renewables to consumers,” she says.

“I asked officials to prove to me that renewables were cheap and then realised their numbers were bogus.”

Coutinho claims Miliband has since cancelled her review. She also says the Energy Secretary is paying too much for net zero.

A spokesman for Miliband rejected the criticisms, instead claiming that solar and wind farms would bring huge benefits. “We are reversing decades of underinvestment and delivering the biggest upgrade to the grid in decades, which will minimise constraint costs, meet the capacity needed to deliver clean power by 2030 and help bring down bills for good,” he said.

He also played down the threat of grid disruption. “The National Energy System Operator has always had to manage changes in demand between the winter and summer months. They have well-established tools to manage the grid, including during times of low demand, which will not impact supply.”

The fresh row over solar is just the latest front in the battle over green energy.

Within Labour, Miliband’s renewables rollout has delighted the party faithful and made him increasingly popular among members.

However, it has also turned energy and climate policy, an area on which there was general political agreement until two years ago, into a culture war.

Leading the charge against Labour is Reform UK, which has pledged to disconnect solar farms if it wins power at the next general election.

“These solar farms are being built for the benefit of foreign investors, but they are destroying our countryside and ruining our environment,” says Richard Tice, Reform’s energy spokesman.

“It’s my living mission to stop this madness. Britain has done its bit – we’ve cut emissions by 50pc and impoverished ourselves doing it. Now’s the time to make Britain prosperous again, and that means cheap energy, lots of it, whether from gas, nuclear or oil.”

No doubt Miliband will disagree. However, the latest warning from Neso may at least make him sit up and notice.

data.title: Reeves promises help on soaring factory energy bills – but only in a year’s time
data.description:
British factories face a year-long wait for relief from soaring energy bills despite Rachel Reeves’s suggestion that help is on the way.

The Chancellor promised on Sunday to “set out the next phase of our plans to boost Britain’s competitiveness” in response to high energy costs, which are worsening because of the war in Iran.

But The Telegraph understands that one of the Government’s key measures to cut the country’s crippling industrial energy bills cannot start any earlier than April 2027.

Business groups urged Ms Reeves to move faster, with a wave of inflation already hitting companies. New surveys of confidence and output suggest both have already fallen to lows not seen since the pandemic.

Addressing the “global economic crisis” triggered by war Iran, the Chancellor wrote over the weekend that she would “set out the principles that will guide how we support businesses in the months ahead”.

Ms Reeves is this week expected to outline details of the British Industrial Competitiveness Scheme (BICS), which will see green levies on electricity costs waived for about 7,000 companies in eight key sectors.

However, a government source indicated that the Chancellor would not bring forward the timelines for the programme to launch. It means factories face a year-long wait for relief at a time when energy costs are soaring.

‘Essential’ to move faster

The Confederation of British Industry (CBI) lobby group said it was “essential” to move faster. “It needs to be delivered at pace. Any delay risks undermining business confidence at a time when it is already under strain,” said Tania Kumar, the CBI’s director for energy transition.

In a survey of 3,000 British voters conducted by the Institute for Economic Affairs, 77pc wanted the Government to focus on cutting the cost of energy. Similarly large majorities urged the Government to focus on economic growth, and cut taxes on workers and businesses.

In an article in The Sunday Times, the Chancellor said she did not want to be rushed into potentially expensive or ineffective policies.

Her hesitancy came as Donald Trump on Sunday said the US Navy would block the Strait of Hormuz – a vital transit route at least one fifth of the world’s oil, gas and fertiliser – to any ship that had paid a toll to Iran.

This could extend a conflict that has already squeezed energy supply chains and driven up fuel and electricity costs. Brent crude prices jumped as high as $104 a barrel on Monday morning after closing on Friday at $94.

Ian Stewart, the Deloitte chief economist, said the Iran war was “reshaping business sentiment” after a survey of finance chiefs by the consultancy found confidence at a six-year low. The barometer fell to -57pc in the first three months of this year, from minus 13pc the previous quarter.

Mr Stewart said: “It’s created a shock to CFO confidence, lowering optimism to levels we haven’t seen since the early days of the Covid-19 pandemic.

“Finance leaders are coping with high levels of external uncertainty, and their focus is on managing risks from geopolitics, rising energy prices and higher financing costs.”

A separate survey of 4,000 businesses by accountancy firm BDO found output had dropped to a five-year low.

“The decline in business output and confidence underlines the UK’s exposure to the global energy shock,” said Scott Knight, head of growth at BDO.

“Confidence will not be restored until energy flows stabilise under a lasting ceasefire.”

BDO warned the combination of increasing price pressures and falling demand would prompt businesses to stop hiring workers, with employment intentions at a 15-year low.

Britain’s manufacturers were already labouring under the highest electricity prices in Europe, but these will rise still further if the Iran conflict keeps squeezing oil and gas supplies.

Ms Kumar said the Chancellor was “right to resist the lure of short-term, unaffordable sticking plasters”, but she needed to seize this moment to make Britain more cost-competitive.

The BICS is a seven-year scheme to exempt businesses from paying two green levies and an energy-security charge. This could reduce their electricity costs by up to £40 per megawatt hour.

Scheme ‘does not go far enough’

To be eligible, factories must be in economically critical sectors. These include advanced manufacturing, clean energy, defence, digital technologies and life sciences. Suppliers to these sectors, such as makers of chemicals, steel or critical minerals, can also qualify.

Responding to a consultation on the BICS that ended in January, the Aluminium Federation said its members had “consistently reported that the proposed April 2027 implementation date is far too late”.

The Chemicals Industry Association told the Government that the scheme was not as generous as others in the EU, and “does not go far enough to make the UK a globally competitive manufacturing location”.

Other business groups supported the BICS but warned the government not to fund the scheme by increasing levies on companies that were not part of it.

Elexon, a company that provides data and settlement services to the energy industry, said getting BICS up and running by next April would be “very challenging”.

Its submission said the Government had to amend laws and build new processes and systems, and much of the groundwork for that would need to be finished by early this summer.

The Treasury declined to comment on speculation that Ms Reeves would this week announce an expansion of the BICS.

data.title: Bibby Stockholm operator admits overcharging the Government £120m
data.description:
The company that ran the Bibby Stockholm asylum barge has admitted overcharging the taxpayer by £118m.

Corporate Travel Management (CTM), an Australian travel business, has raised its estimate of how much it owes the Government from £77m to the new total after a “forensic” investigation into “erroneous billing activity”.

In a statement to the stock exchange in Australia, CTM said it was now “negotiating commercial arrangements” with the Government to refund the money.

The company has been under a cloud for almost eight months, since its stock was suspended from exchange trading because of irregularities in its UK accounts.

CTM posted the results of KPMG’s forensic review into the issues on Wednesday, which focused on a 2021 deal to provide hotel quarantines during the Covid pandemic.

KPMG’s review initially found CTM had charged the Government almost £55m more for the quarantine hotel rooms than it was paying the hotels.

In its statement, the company said this had been inadvertent rather than intentional.

That discovery prompted a wider investigation, which found other instances where CTM had overcharged, or retained funds that should have been repaid or passed on. This pushed the total to £118m.

Some of the initial amount was supposed to be repaid under a deal CTM supposedly struck with the British Government in 2023. But, CTM said the Government now claimed to have no record of this deal.

CTM’s British boss sacked

The company sacked its British chief executive in December and said on Wednesday that it had overhauled the leadership, culture, organisation and processes of its UK operation.

“The measures we have taken in the UK business to date have been extensive and thorough, and we will continue to take the necessary action,” said Ana Pedersen, CTM’s chief executive.

CTM said it had already paid back £12m. The company said it hoped to negotiate a phased repayment plan with the British Government, with further refunds coming “over an extended period”.

A Home Office spokesman said that “an internal investigation is continuing into this overspend”.

The spokesman did not comment on how much more would be repaid and when, nor on whether CTM would continue providing services to the Government. CTM still has a £550m Home Office contract to provide hotel rooms for asylum claimants.

During the past decade, CTM became one of the Government’s go-to suppliers of accommodation and relocation services.

It was involved in repatriating stranded Britons during the early days of the Covid pandemic, then won a contract to manage the hotel quarantine system during the 2021 Covid lockdown.

After running the Bibby Stockholm barge off the Dorset coast, CTM won a four-year, £550m contract in April last year to find hotels for asylum seekers, including allocating rooms and organising transport and meals.

data.title: AstraZeneca to invest £11bn in China after snubbing Britain
data.description:
AstraZeneca has unveiled plans to invest $15bn (£11bn) in China just months after dropping hundreds of millions of pounds of investment in Britain.

The Cambridge-based company announced the investment, which will be used to expand its medical manufacturing and research capabilities, during Sir Keir Starmer’s visit to Beijing. The Prime Minister hailed the financing as a deal that would help to support British jobs back home.

But the announcement follows a period of frosty relations between the Government and AstraZeneca which has seen the drugmaker threaten to halt investment in Britain in a row over NHS pricing.

The pharmaceutical giant, which is Britain’s biggest listed company, has abandoned plans for a £200m expansion of its Cambridge research site and £450m overhaul of its Liverpool vaccine factory.

The deal may also raise eyebrows given that five of AstraZeneca’s staff were detained by authorities in China in 2024 amid claims of potential illegal activity. The president of its China division was subsequently placed under investigation by local police, with no further details provided.

Astra is the largest drugs company in China, which is the company’s second-biggest market after the US.

Separately, AstraZeneca also announced on Friday that it had struck a deal worth as much as $18.2bn with China’s CSPC Pharmaceuticals.

Under the tie-up, AstraZeneca will have the global rights to sell CSPC’s obesity drugs outside China.

CSPC is currently developing once-monthly weight-loss jabs. The biggest weight-loss treatments currently require people to inject themselves once a week.

In total, if all of CSPC’s eight obesity drugs make it to market, AstraZeneca said the deal could be worth as much as $18.2bn – including future sales of the weight-loss treatments.

Sir Pascal Soriot, the company’s chief executive, is one of a clutch of Western business leaders who are occasionally summoned for meetings with Xi Jinping, the Chinese premier.

Sir Pascal said on Thursday: “Today’s landmark investment of $15bn begins an exciting next chapter for AstraZeneca in China, which has become a critical contributor to scientific innovation, advanced manufacturing and global public health.”

The “landmark” move was recognition of China’s growing scientific capabilities in areas that could help unlock new treatments for cancer, haematological conditions and autoimmune diseases, among others. It will be focused on sites in Bejing, Shanghai, Wuxi, Taizhou and Qingdao.

Sir Keir said: “Unlocking opportunities for British businesses across the globe and delivering for working people back home is always the driving force behind my international engagements.

“AstraZeneca’s expansion and leadership in China will help the British manufacturer continue to grow – supporting thousands of UK jobs.”

Astra has been at loggerheads with the Government over how much the NHS pays for medicines.

The company and its rivals have argued that the way the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (Nice) evaluates “value for money” on medicines is flawed and deprives patients of potential breakthrough treatments.

For instance, Nice previously rejected Enhertu, Astra’s breast cancer drug.

Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, walked away from talks with drugmakers last summer after failing to strike an agreement.

But in December, ministers announced a new deal with the US that saw Nice agree to pay up to 25pc more for treatments, a move aimed at protecting the UK life sciences industry.

data.title: Hope for run-down Boots as billionaire owner’s wife exits
data.description:
The British high street is experiencing its biggest cull in years.

Already reeling from a series of costly tax raids at the hands of Rachel Reeves, tough Christmas trading has proven to be the nail in the coffin for a string of struggling household names, including Claire’s, Russell & Bromley and LK Bennett. More high-profile casualties are expected in the coming weeks.

Amid the continued hollowing out of town centres, one high street stalwart remains: Boots.

Outposts of the lipstick-to-prescriptions business can still be found across the country, but many are in a sad state. After its exit from the stock market in 2007, Boots has spent the last two decades being starved of investment.

With serial dealmaker Stefano Pessina leading the charge, Boots has been allowed to dwindle – first under the ownership of private equity’s original Barbarians at the Gate, KKR, then as a forgotten overseas outpost of American pharmacy giant Walgreens.

Now Boots is in the hands of another private equity house, Sycamore Partners, after the New York-based outfit took Walgreens off the public markets last summer. Employees must fear yet more decline.

However, a shake-up at the very top of the company may signal a revival for one of Britain’s best-loved and most important retailers.

After leading the pharmacy giant for almost a decade, Boots chief executive Ornella Barra is stepping down to become chair. She replaces Pessina, who is also her business partner and husband, and is believed to still own half of the business at the ripe age of 84.

At the same time, managing director Anthony Hemmerdinger is being handed a much bigger role running Boots, including oversight of the No7 beauty brand – long regarded as one of several jewels in the stable.

The management reshuffle is viewed internally, and in the City, as another major step towards setting Boots free from its ownership purgatory and returning it to the UK stock market.

City sources said a float is still the main long-term ambition of the owners, and more likely to occur next year than in 2026.

A former executive said a spin-off of Boots “was always the plan” and the boardroom changes were “a step towards that”. The break-up of Walgreens into five standalone entities – Boots among them – shortly after its £8bn takeover last year was seen as the first real step.

The prospect of Boots being groomed for a spectacular public markets comeback under new leadership will raise hopes of a long overdue makeover of its unloved shops. Shoppers will be hoping that it also means a halt to any further closures.

The sale to Sycamore came after Boots axed 300 stores across the UK, drastically reducing its footprint from 2,200 to around 1,900 branches. Boots also shut 48 opticians in 2020, and had kickstarted a store closure plan the previous year that ended with more than 200 outlets being given the chop.

The status of Boots as more than just your typical bog-standard retailer means its future garners more attention than others.

Armed with Britain’s best-selling skincare brand No 7, the chain believes it can continue to compete in the booming beauty market despite the excitement surrounding the arrival of LVMH-backed French phenomenon Sephora in Britain, as well as other upstarts.

Amid the glamour of expensive lipstick and eyelash-thickening mascara, it is easy to forget that Boots is an integral part of the country’s healthcare system. Without its sprawling network of pharmacists, thousands of people would struggle to get their vital medicines and daily pills.

Its last round of store closures was accompanied by an apologetic statement explaining that the decision was “not taken lightly” and that in all cases there was an alternative store less than three miles away.

Yet that didn’t prevent an outcry in some affected towns, with locals complaining about being forced to endure long queues at other pharmacies, which in some cases had led to delays in getting their prescriptions.

Boots bosses have long rejected the portrayal of a business stuck in retreat as lazy and outdated.

“The Boots Group is in great shape, both financially and operationally, with Boots UK having grown market share for 19 consecutive quarters,” Barra said as she stepped aside.

But that fact masks the two-speed nature of the business. A former executive said there are around 500 Boots shops that are “the powerhouse of the business” – the vast majority of which have already been revamped.

However, the rest are much smaller shops, which are vital to the surrounding communities but cost “between £50,000 and £100,000” to refurbish with no resulting “uplift” in turnover, making it hard to justify the outlay.

“Pharmacies are really difficult to make money in now, because of what the Government pays per prescription,” another ex-director said. In November, the Department of Health and Social Care announced that NHS prescription charges would be frozen for the second year running.

 Clive Black, analyst at Shore Capital, adds: “There has been immense profiling of a small number of new and modernised stores, gaining lots of capital expenditure and senior management attention, to show the newfound vitality of Boots.

“But the reality is that many Boots are drab, under-invested, and needing of an injection of elevated execution that British shoppers will notice across the board.”

Those who know the business well say Boots branches need to become even bigger healthcare hubs, so that they can alleviate pressure on local GPs by providing more of the services that you would usually go to your doctor for.

The chain already does private online doctor consultations, vaccinations and blood pressure checks, as well as specialised treatments for skin and hair loss. However, it is eyeing a larger role in diagnostics with “at-home” testing kits for conditions such as high cholesterol, vitamin deficiencies and food intolerances. These will be offered under the mantra of standing “shoulder to shoulder with the NHS”.

If the move is successful, it would justify spending more capital on its smaller stores, many of which struggle to turn a profit.

The former executive says: “The mantra…was always ‘We’re going to have a great Boots in every major town, and none that we’re ashamed of.’ I think that will continue.”

data.title: Trump is an utter disaster for the world’s oil and gas industry
data.description:
The bittersweet joke in energy circles is that the Nobel committee should create a new climate prize to reward Donald Trump for his outstanding services.

Nobody has done more to accelerate the world’s electrotech revolution and pull forward the irreversible collapse of the oil and gas industry – not even Greta Thunberg, Al Gore or the strategic planners of the Chinese Communist Party.

The Carlyle Group made waves last year with a report called the “New Joule Order”. The giant US investment firm argued that seaborne molecules were no longer safe in the Hobbesian new world of America First and maritime anarchy.

Jeff Currie, its energy guru, said the open trading system of the last 80 years had been a function of American and allied power, relying on “the US navy as its muscle”.

This gift to the world, akin to the role of Britain’s Royal Navy in the 19th century, had allowed a constant stream of tankers carrying oil, diesel, naphtha, and liquefied natural gas (LNG) to criss-cross the oceans with scarcely a moment’s thought about choke points, taking for granted the international dollar system that lubricated trade so efficiently.

Currie said the “quest for security” was becoming the dominant driver of electrotech, as countries rushed to insulate themselves from geopolitical risk. It has little to do with climate change and even less to do with the red herring of net zero.

But not even he expected the US to lose its strategic compass so comprehensively as to attack Iran without securing the Strait of Hormuz, cutting off 8pc of total world trade for 48 days, including supplies of fertilisers, helium needed for semiconductors and a host of critical inputs that Washington forgot to think about.

Currie says the elemental contract of the petrodollar system is, or was, that the US would keep the sea lanes open and protect oil and gas producers from all foes. In return, the petrostates would recycle their vast earnings into US Treasuries and Wall Street, letting America enjoy the exorbitant privilege of inexhaustible cheap capital. “The grand bargain has been violated,” he said.

You might think that US oil and gas companies would be celebrating the windfall profits of Trump’s war, but the mood was closer to horror at the recent CeraWeek summit in Houston, the sanctum sanctorum of the drilling industry.

Casey Merriman, from Energy Intelligence, said the mood behind the scenes was “panic and dread” rooted in an awful premonition that the fallout of war would pose “existential risks to the future of oil and gas”.

Data from Ember show that 78pc of humanity lives in countries that are net importers of oil. These states have been bleeding $1.7tn (£1.25tn) a year just to pay oil rents to petrostates or frackers in Texas, and that could double again in short order if oil futures converge upwards to reflect the $140-$150 levels seen in the physical markets.

The importers have put up with this glaring anomaly out of inertia and the well-organised resistance of vested interests. Not any longer.

“Depending on fossil fuels is dangerous,” said Lee Jae-myung, the South Korean president, confessing that matters were now so grave that he could not sleep.

He has called for a fundamental rethink of South Korea’s energy strategy. “This is an emergency situation and we must shift to renewable energy very quickly,” he said.

France is doubling investment in electrification and will ban boilers in new homes at the end of this year. Variants of this debate are going on all over the world.

The double crisis of Trump’s war so soon after Vladimir Putin’s war has shaken the world out of complacency. All can now see that America has become a predator state. They can also see that a drone costing $20,000 is enough to stop a $150m tanker dead in the water.

Kingsmill Bond, Ember’s director of energy, said this shock had hit just as electrotech comes of age and is undercutting legacy fossil fuels on pure cost for power, heat and most land transport across the majority of the world’s populated regions.

“We didn’t have an alternative in past crises. Now we do. We have a suite of completely new solutions that change everything,” he said.

“Solar power coupled with storage is down to $60 [per] MWh across the global sunbelt. It costs $160 [per] MWh right now to cover just the cost of importing variable gas in Asia. It’s game over for LNG in Asia,” he said.

The figures for total net fossil imports as a share of primary energy are revealing, and not what you might think. To pick a few, they are: Japan (84pc), South Korea (80pc), Italy (72pc), Turkey and Spain (70pc), Germany (67pc), France (47pc), India and the UK (37pc) and China (24pc).

Britain is partially shielded by North Sea oil and gas working in tandem with fast-increasing volumes of North Sea wind power. The International Monetary Fund persists in claiming that this country is more vulnerable than peers to a fossil shock. It might usefully revisit its assumptions and stop repeating arrant nonsense.

Bond said the lesson of the two Opec crises in the 1970s is that it took the second shock in 1979 to cause a lasting shift in policy. “Global final oil demand per capita peaked in 1979 and has never recovered,” he said.

The response after the 1970s was to switch to more fuel-efficient cars and break the link between oil and GDP growth, but it could only go so far. This time the wider fossil fuel system faces terminal run-off in the importing countries, swept away by the scramble for safer homemade supply, which now also happens to be cheaper. Obviously, oil and gas will still be needed for jet fuel (for a while) and as a feedstock for petrochemicals.

China is already a post-oil, post-gas, electrostate economy, rolling out more wind, solar and electric vehicles each year than the rest of the world combined – although it has not cracked solid-state batteries for EVs and may yet be outflanked by the West.

What began as a strategic insurance policy against a future US naval blockade of China’s seaborne energy imports has become a juggernaut by now. Sales of EVs and plug-in hybrids were half of the Chinese car market last year. They are running at 60pc this month.

The Chinese near-abroad is now slotting into this new joule order. EV sales are around 40pc in Vietnam and Singapore, 25pc in Thailand, 18pc in Indonesia, and rising.

Laurence Tubiana, the chief convenor of the Paris Climate Accord, said that as recently as five years ago African leaders would bridle at green colonialism, accusing the West of trying to deny the Global South a cheap energy route out of poverty.

Today the mood is radically different. Country after country has done its sums and concluded that it could leapfrog centralised, fossil-based grids and opt instead for solar micro-grids, slashing costs and freeing itself of fuel-import bondage. “They have no interest any longer in being trapped in the old energy system,” she said.

We have not felt the full impact of this crisis yet. Currie said it reminded him of the eerie insouciance in early days of the pandemic. First it hit China and shut the economy: the world reacted as if the virus had nothing to do with them. Then it hit South Korea, then Iran, then Italy, and still stock markets kept rising. It took six weeks before Wall Street suddenly buckled and crashed 35pc along with a seizure in the US Treasury market.

Trump’s war will hit home once the physical barrels stop arriving and cannot be bought for love or money, a moment scheduled for late April once the usable global buffer of 800 million barrels is exhausted.

Currie has another warning as the S&P 500 reached new highs based on nothing more than Truth Social tweets. The US oil and gas companies make up just 3pc of the equity capitalisation of the index; the other 97pc is on the wrong side of the Gulf War trade and will soon suffer the consequences of what remains the biggest energy shock of modern times.

Wars have a bad habit of spinning out of control.
data.author: The telegraph
